claim under Section 8 is moot and (2) a company violates Section 8 by appointing a natural 
person to serve on the board of a competing company.  This statement also explains how 
interlocking board member arrangements can undermine fair competition and therefore violate 
the FTC Act and, by extension, California’s UCL.  Finally, this statement discusses the legal 
standard for when group boycotts can run afoul of Section 1.  
The Agencies take no position on any other issue in this case, including the facts alleged 
by Plaintiffs. 
FACTUAL BACKGROUND 
The First Amended Complaint, ECF No. 32, alleges that “Artificial General 
Intelligence,” i.e., “a machine having intelligence for a wide variety of tasks like a human,” 
poses certain risks. Am. Compl. ¶¶ 4, 74-75. To  address these risks, Plaintiff Musk helped 
establish OpenAI in 2015. Am. Compl. ¶ 88. That same year, OpenAI incorporated as a 
“nonprofit corporation” committed to the “research, development and distribution of technology 
related to artificial intelligence.”  Am. Compl. ¶ 89.   
The First Amended Complaint further alleges that (1) defendant Reid Hoffman 
simultaneously served on OpenAI’s and Microsoft’s boards of directors from March 2017 to 
March 2023, and (2) defendant Deannah Templeton simultaneously served as Vice President of 
Partnerships and Operations in the Technology & Research division at Microsoft and as a non-
voting member of OpenAI’s board of directors from November 2023 until July 2024.  Am. 
Compl. ¶¶ 163, 371; see also id. ¶¶ 168, 331(m), 374. 
Based on these allegations, and others, Plaintiffs bring 26 separate claims including 
breach of contract, fraud, false advertising, breach of fiduciary duty, and claims under the 
Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act.  Plaintiffs also allege Defendants violated 
the Sherman and Clayton Acts based in part on conduct affecting the market for “generative AI 
models and platforms.” Am. Compl. ¶ 204. 
On November 29, 2024, Plaintiffs filed a Motion for a Preliminary Injunction seeking to 
enjoin Defendants from, among other things, “directly or indirectly undertaking any action for 
the purpose of or tending to have the effect of, interlocking directorates or benefitting from

ARGUMENT 
The Agencies offer their views on the proper application of Section 8 of the Clayton Act, 
Section 1 of the Sherman Act, and the UCL to the specific issues discussed below.  The 
Agencies take no position on the ultimate disposition of the motion. 
I. Section 8’s Bar on Interlocking Directorates 
A. Statutory Background 
Section 8 of the Clayton Act provides that “[n]o person shall, at the same time, serve as a 
director or officer in any two corporations . . . that are . . . competitors such that the elimination 
of competition by agreement between them would constitute a violation of any of the antitrust 
laws.” 15 U.S.C. § 19. Section 8 makes interloc king directorates, i.e., overlapping directors or 
officers among competing companies, per se unlawful.  TRW, Inc. v. FTC, 647 F.2d 942, 948 
(9th Cir. 1981). At the same time, this broad prohibition is subject to certain limitations and 
exceptions. See, e.g., 15 U.S.C. § 19(a)(2), (b).  For example, the companies must be engaged in 
interstate commerce and have “capital, surplus, and undivided profits aggregating more than” 
$48,559,000. Id.  Section 8 also does not apply if competitive sales: (A) of either corporation are 
less than $4,855,900; (B) of either corporation are less than two percent of the corporation’s total 
sales; or (C) of each corporation are less than four percent of the corporation’s total sales.  Id. at 
§ 8(a)(2).  Finally, Section 19(b) grants a one-year grace period to individuals who were eligible 
to serve at the time of election but a change in circumstances later created an illegal interlock.  
The purpose of Section 8’s prohibition on interlocking directorates is “to nip in the bud 
incipient antitrust violations by removing the opportunity or temptation for such violations.”  
TRW, 647 F.2d at, 946–47.  Put another way, Section 8 “prevent[s] restraints on competition 
before they materialize[] by outlawing a particular practice thought to facilitate such restraints.”  
Id. at 948; see also Reading Int’l, Inc. v. Oaktree Cap. Mgmt. LLC, 317 F. Supp. 2d 301, 330 
(S.D.N.Y. 2003). For example, preventing interl ocking directorates helps “avoid the opportunity

for the coordination of business decisions by competitors and . . . the exchange of commercially 
sensitive information by competitors.” Square D Co. v. Schneider S.A., 760 F. Supp. 362, 366 
(S.D.N.Y. 1991). Such conduct can facilitate unr easonable restraints of trade in violation of 
Section 1 of the Sherman Act (15 U.S.C. § 1) or help a monopolist maintain and entrench its 
power in violation of Section 2 of the Sherman Act (15 U.S.C. § 2).  In this way, Section 8 
supports and reinforces the federal antitrust laws more generally.  See United States v. Sears, 
Roebuck & Co., 111 F. Supp. 614, 616 (S.D.N.Y. 1953) (“Section 8 was but one of a series of 
measures which finally emerged as the Clayton Act, all intended to strengthen the Sherman 
Act.”). 
Courts have consistently interpreted Section 8 in view of these “prophylactic and 
remedial purposes.” Square D Co. v. Schneider S.A., 760 F. Supp. 362, 366 (S.D.N.Y. 1991). 
For example, the Ninth Circuit has held that establishing a Section 8 violation does not require 
“proof that the interlock has an actual anticompetitive effect.” TRW, 647 F.2d at 946–47 (citing 
Protectoseal Co. v. Barancik, 484 F.2d 585, 589 (7th Cir. 1973) (Stevens, J.)). “On the contrary, 
the statute reflects a public interest in preventing directors from serving in positions which 
involve either a potential conflict of interest or a potential frustration of competition.”  
Protectoseal, 484 F.2d at 589. Likewise, courts have refuse d to read the statute so rigidly that a 
company can evade Section 8 liability through tactics that elevate form over substance, e.g., “by 
calling its agents on the competitors’ board something other than either officers or directors.”  
Square, 760 F. Supp. at 366; Reading, 317 F. Supp. 2d at 327–28 (rejecting reading of Section 8 
that would “elevate form over substance” and render the statue “a formalism”). 
B. Section 8 Claims Are Not Mooted by Simply Unwinding an Interlock  
Defendants argue “there is no live ‘case or controversy’” here because “neither Hoffman 
nor Templeton is currently affiliated with OpenAI’s board in any capacity.”  ECF No. 64 at 13. 
But ending an interlocking directorate, e.g., by having a person resign from a corporate board, is 
not sufficient, on its own, to moot a claim under Section 8 of the Clayton Act.  See United States 
v. W. T. Grant Co., 345 U.S. 629, 632 (1953); TRW, 647 F.2d at 953. In resolving this matter, 
the Court should avoid holding otherwise.

“[A]s a general rule, ‘voluntary cessation of allegedly illegal conduct does not deprive the 
tribunal of power to hear and determine the case, i.e., does not make the case moot.’”  Cty. of Los 
Angeles v. Davis, 440 U.S. 625, 631 (1979). Otherwise “[a] defendant [would be] free to return 
to his old ways,” and that, “together with a public interest in having the legality of the [disputed] 
practices settled, militates against a mootness conclusion.”  United States v. W. T. Grant Co., 345 
U.S. 629, 632 (1953). Thus, where a defendant volunt arily ceases illegal conduct, it still bears a 
“heavy burden” to demonstrate it is “absolutely clear that the allegedly wrongful behavior could 
not reasonably be expected to recur.” TRW, 647 F.2d at 953 (quoting United States v. 
Concentrated Phosphate Export Ass’n, 393 U.S. 199, 203 (1968)). Additionally, the relevant 
“concern is with repeated violations of the same law, and not merely with repetition of the same 
offensive conduct.” Id. 
The issue of mootness in the context of Section 8 was addressed squarely by the Ninth 
Circuit in TRW. 647 F.2d at 953. There, the interlocking director (i) resigned his position at the 
competing company, (ii) “decided not to stand for reelection,” (iii) and “provided sworn 
assurances that [he] would avoid offensive directorships in the future.”  Id. The Ninth Circuit 
still held the case was not moot.  Id. As the Ninth Circuit expl ained, mootness depends on the 
risk that a defendant may violate Section 8 in the future, not simply whether it might resume the 
specific interlock at issue.  Id. That risk may remain even af ter an interlock is unwound despite 
“good faith . . . assurances of future compliance.” Id. 
Moreover, “a potential conflict of interest or a potential frustration of competition,” 
Protectoseal, 484 F.2d at 589, may remain even after an interlock is resolved and even if one is 
unlikely to recur. For example, if compan ies use interlocking directors to exchange 
competitively sensitive information, see Square, 760 F. Supp. at 367, they may frustrate 
competition so long as the information remains sensitive and in their possession.  Thus, holding 
that a company moots a potential claim under Section 8 by simply ending an interlock would be 
contrary to the purpose of the statute and would significantly undermine enforcement of the 
Clayton Act. Cf. United States v. Paramount Pictures, 334 U.S. 131, 171–72 (1948) (“[T]he 
requirement that the defendants restore what they unlawfully obtained [through an unlawful

Unwound interlocks are subject to the same mootness analysis as other abandoned per se conduct 
such as market allocation or price fixing.  W. T. Grant Co., 345 U.S. at 632 (voluntary cessation 
of Section 8 interlock does not deprive the tribunal of power to hear and determine the case);

combination . . . or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce’ which § 1 of the Act strikes 
down, whether the concerted activity be wholly nascent or abortive on the one hand, or 
successful on the other.”) (citation omitted). 
Accordingly, if a plaintiff properly pleads a likelihood of recurrence or an ongoing harm 
through the wrongful retention of competitively sensitive information obtained through the 
alleged interlocks, Section 8 claims are not moot.  See TRW, 647 F.2d at 953. Even in the face of 
good faith assurances of future compliance with Section 8, a court may find that “the heavy 
burden of proof regarding mootness has not been satisfied.”  Id. 
C. Section 8 Bars Relationships that Create an Interlock Regardless of Form  
It is well-established that an individual or a company can violate Section 8 when it 
“serve[s] as a director or officer” directly or indirectly, e.g., through an agent, deputy, or 
representative. For example, in TRW, the Ninth Circuit held that “section 8 prohibits 
corporations from choosing, and natural persons from serving as, directors in violation of section 
8’s substantive requirements.” Id. at 949. Consistent with TRW, if a company chooses a natural 
person to serve on a competitor’s board as its agent, the company and the natural person violate 
Section 8 unless an exception or safe harbor applies.

California v. Fed. Power Comm’n, 369 U.S. 482, 485 (1962) (“Immunity from the antitrust laws 
is not lightly implied.”). Cons equently, an individual cannot evade liability by serving as an 
“observer” on a competitor’s board.  Similarly, companies cannot evade liability by relying on an 
agent to serve on a competitor’s board. In resolving this matter, the Court should avoid holding 
otherwise. 
A board “observer” may be a “director” under 15 U.S.C. § 19 in certain circumstances.  
Specifically, a company or individual cannot use an indirect means to a prohibited end, such as 
by asking another person to serve as a board observer to obtain entry to a meeting that is 
otherwise off limits due to Section 8’s ban on interlocks.  Such misdirection would undermine 
Section 8’s intent to impose a clear ban on direct involvement in the management of a 
competitor. Accordingly, the Court should determine whether Defendants violated Section 8 
based on case-specific allegations without “exalt[ing] form over substance,” Square, 760 F. 
Supp. at 366, or disturbing longstanding precedent holding that a company may violate Section 8 
when it appoints an agent, deputy, or representative to serve on the board of a competitor, see 
TRW, 647 F.2d at 949; see also Square, 760 F. Supp. at 367 (“[A] cause of action under § 8 is 
stated where a company attempts to place on the Board of a competitor individuals who are 
agents of, and have an employment or business relationship with, such company.”); Reading, 
317 F. Supp. 2d at 331-32 (similar).

Interlocking directorates are an unfair method of competition under federal antitrust law, 
including the FTC Act.  Courts have looked to Section 5 of the FTC Act and its prohibition 
Ohio 1974), aff’d, 513 F.2d 633 (6th Cir. 1975).  In each case, however, the key question is

director’ of [the competing company].” Reading, 317 F. Supp. 2d at 331–32; see also Blau v.

purposes of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934], if . . . Lehman actually functioned as a 
director through Thomas, who had been deputized by Lehman to perform a director’s duties not 
for himself but for Lehman.”).

III. Group Boycotts Under Section 1 
Plaintiffs seek to enjoin an alleged “fund-no-competitors” edict, which they unify under a 
group boycott theory of antitrust, in violation of Section 1 of the Sherman Act.  The Agencies 
take no position on the alleged facts but file this statement in order to clarify the proper legal 
analysis for group boycotts.
5  Section 1 of the Sherman Act forbids “[e]very contract, 
combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade.” 15 U.S.C. § 1.  
A claim under Section 1 therefore requires two key elements: (1) “a contract, combination, or 
conspiracy”—i.e., “concerted action”— (2) that “unreasonably restrains trade.”  Am. Needle, Inc. 
v. Nat’l Football League, 560 U.S. 183, 186 (2010). Concerted action can be unreasonable in 
one of two ways. Ohio v. Am. Express Co., 585 U.S. 529, 540 (2018). First, it may be 
unreasonable per se, without any further inquiry into its competitive effects or justifications, 
because of its inherently anticompetitive “nature and character.”  Standard Oil Co. of New Jersey 
v. United States, 221 U.S. 1, 64 (1911). Typically, only ho rizontal restraints, i.e., concerted 
action between actual or potential competitors, are per se unlawful.  Am. Express, 585 U.S. at 
541. Second, a restraint may be unreasonable under the “rule of reason,” a “fact-specific 
assessment” of challenged conduct’s “effect on competition.”  Id. (internal quotation marks 
omitted). Group boycotts involving competing firm s can properly be analyzed under the per se 
rule. See Honey Bum, LLC v. Fashion Nova, Inc., 63 F.4th 813, 820–21 (9th Cir. 2023). 
 The litigants disagree about whether the alleged fund-no-competitors edict is horizontal 
or vertical.6  This distinction does not preclude application of Section 1.  Without commenting

unregulated merger.” The charac terization of the relationship between the entities for antitrust 
purposes is a factual inquiry on which the Agencies take no position at this time. 
6 To the extent Defendants dispute the existence of concerted action, i.e., a group boycott against 
OpenAI’s rivals, the Court would err if it required Plaintiffs to establish the existence of a

on the facts as alleged, a group boycott can have a vertical aspect; and per se liability under 
Section 1 can attach so long as there is also a horizontal element to the alleged conspiracy.  See 
NYNEX Corp. v. Discon, Inc., 525 U.S. 128, 136 (1998) (surveying precedent and distinguishing 
purely vertical agreement between supplier and customer from cases also involving a horizontal 
agreement among competitors). Indeed, “[t]he horizontal agreement can exist either among the 
initiators of the boycott (as in Fashion Originators) or those pressured into joining (as in 
Klor’s).” Honey Bum, 63 F.4th at 821. Thus, a conspiracy  among competing firms can be per se 
unlawful even if it is organized by one of their customers, as is the case with some hub-and-
spoke conspiracies. E.g., Klor’s Inc. v. Broadway-Hale Stores, Inc., 359 U.S. 207, 211 (1959); 
Toys “R” Us, Inc. v. FTC, 221 F.3d 928, 936–38 (7th Cir. 2000); United States v. Apple, Inc., 
791 F.3d 290, 323 (2d Cir. 2015). For example, in Apple, a price fixing conspiracy was 
condemned as per se unlawful even though it was organized by a distributor (Apple) that did not 
compete with the other co-conspirators (book publishers).  791 F.3d at 323 (2d Cir. 2015) 
(anticompetitive “threat is just as significant when a vertical market participant organizes the 
conspiracy”). 
Moreover, group boycotts are “not to be tolerated merely because the victim is just one 
merchant whose business is so small that his destruction makes little difference to the economy.” 
Klor’s, 359 U.S. at 213 (“Monopoly can as surely thrive by the elimination of such small 
businessmen, one at a time, as it can by driving them out in large groups”).  And the fact that 
alternative resources may be secured by the boycotted firm beyond those supplied by the 
boycotting group cannot serve as a defense. See PLS.com, 32 F.4th at 835 (“[A] group of 
competitors coercing a competitor’s suppliers . . . constitutes a group boycott even if the 
competitors do not completely cut off the competitor’s access to inputs it needs.”); Klor’s, 359 
“formal agreement” between members of the alleged boycott.  Am. Tobacco v. United States, 328 
U.S. 781, 809 (1946). A “tacit” agreement can also qualify.  Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 
U.S. 544, 553 (2007); see White v. R.M. Packer Co., 635 F.3d 571, 576 (1st Cir. 2011) (A tacit 
agreement is “one in which only the conspirators’ actions . . . indicate [its] existence.”).  For 
example, concerted action may be inferred if one firm invites others to participate in a boycott 
and then those firms engage in conduct showing acceptance.  PLS.com v. Nat’l Ass’n of Realtors, 
32 F.4th 824, 843 (9th Cir. 2022) (citing Interstate Cir. v. United States, 306 U.S. 208, 227 
(1939)).

Musk v. Altman
is an ongoing lawsuit in the
United States District Court for the Northern District of California
in which
Elon Musk
accuses
OpenAI
and its executives, including CEO
Sam Altman
, of violating the company's founding agreement by prioritizing profits over AI safety.
Background
[
edit
]
This section
needs expansion
. You can help by
adding missing information
.
(
April 2026
)
Musk and Altman are co-founders of OpenAI, which was founded in 2015. Musk left in 2018 in the aftermath of a
power struggle
.
[
1
]
Timeline
[
edit
]
Leadup (2024–2026)
[
edit
]
The lawsuit was originally filed on February 29, 2024.
[
2
]
[
3
]
[
4
]
[
5
]
In November 2024, Musk filed a motion for
preliminary injunction
to block OpenAI from changing from a nonprofit to a for-profit.
He believes the conversion violates the terms of his contributions, which total $44 million, to OpenAI from 2016 to 2020.
[
6
]
In February 2025, United States District Judge
Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers
said that Musk's claim of irreparable harm was a stretch.
[
7
]
In April 2025, twelve former employees of OpenAI stated in an amicus brief that OpenAI had abandoned its nonprofit roots and that Altman "was a person of low integrity who had directly lied to employees about the extent of his knowledge and involvement in OpenAI’s practices of forcing departing employees to sign lifetime non-disparagement agreements." The motion was filed by Harvard law professor
Lawrence Lessig
.
[
8
]
In April 2025, OpenAI countersued Musk, claiming that his actions were deliberate tactics to slow OpenAI in order to benefit his own interests.
[
9
]
That month, the California attorney general declined to join Musk's lawsuit.
[
10
]
On May 1, 2025, Judge
Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers
trimmed the lawsuit, excluding claims of false advertising and breach of fiduciary duty, but allowed claims of fraud and unjust enrichment to proceed.
[
11
]
On May 5, 2025, OpenAI announced that it was no longer planning to restructure into a for-profit company separate from its nonprofit board. Later that day, Musk's lawyer
Marc Toberoff
, announced that the lawsuit against OpenAI would continue.
[
12
]
In October 2025, OpenAI formed a for-profit company –  OpenAI Group PBC – with the original non-profit receiving 26 percent ownership in the new entity and Microsoft receiving a 27 percent stake.
[
13
]
As part of the case files disclosed by Musk's lawyers in federal court in November 2025, Altman was shown to have reached out to
Shivon Zilis
on February 9, 2023, asking her advice about whether to publicly praise Musk on X, which he later did.
[
14
]
In April 2026, Musk amended the lawsuit asking that any monetary damages he might be awarded be given to OpenAI's charity and that Altman be removed from OpenAI's board.
[
15
]
In addition to Altman, various other OpenAI executives, including OpenAI president
Greg Brockman
, are being sued by Musk in the lawsuit as well.

----- SOURCE: openai_charter -----
Title: OpenAI Charter
URL: https://openai.com/charter/
Type: primary_source_charter
Description: OpenAI's published charter, central to the breach-of-mission claim.

OpenAI
Company
OpenAI Charter
Our Charter describes the principles we use to execute on OpenAI’s mission.
This document reflects the strategy we’ve refined over the past two years, including feedback from many people internal and external to OpenAI. The timeline to AGI remains uncertain, but our Charter will guide us in acting in the best interests of humanity throughout its development.
OpenAI’s mission is to ensure that artificial general intelligence (AGI)—by which we mean highly autonomous systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work—benefits all of humanity. We will attempt to directly build safe and beneficial AGI, but will also consider our mission fulfilled if our work aids others to achieve this outcome. To that end, we commit to the following principles:
Broadly distributed benefits
We commit to use any influence we obtain over AGI’s deployment to ensure it is used for the benefit of all, and to avoid enabling uses of AI or AGI that harm humanity or unduly concentrate power.
Our primary fiduciary duty is to humanity. We anticipate needing to marshal substantial resources to fulfill our mission, but will always diligently act to minimize conflicts of interest among our employees and stakeholders that could compromise broad benefit.
Long-term safety
We are committed to doing the research required to make AGI safe, and to driving the broad adoption of such research across the AI community.
We are concerned about late-stage AGI development becoming a competitive race without time for adequate safety precautions. Therefore, if a value-aligned, safety-conscious project comes close to building AGI before we do, we commit to stop competing with and start assisting this project. We will work out specifics in case-by-case agreements, but a typical triggering condition might be “a better-than-even chance of success in the next two years.”
Technical leadership
To be effective at addressing AGI’s impact on society, OpenAI must be on the cutting edge of AI capabilities—policy and safety advocacy alone would be insufficient.
We believe that AI will have broad societal impact before AGI, and we’ll strive to lead in those areas that are directly aligned with our mission and expertise.
Cooperative orientation
We will actively cooperate with other research and policy institutions; we seek to create a global community working together to address AGI’s global challenges.
We are committed to providing public goods that help society navigate the path to AGI. Today this includes publishing most of our AI research, but we expect that safety and security concerns will reduce our traditional publishing in the future, while increasing the importance of sharing safety, policy, and standards research.
----- END SOURCE: openai_charter -----

=== END MATERIALS ===

First task. Read the materials in full. Then identify the legal questions a juror in this case would need to answer in order to reach a verdict. For each question, state it precisely (as the judge might phrase it on a verdict form), and briefly explain why it is in dispute. Do not yet take a position on any question.
transcript.assistant:
# Legal Questions for the Jury Verdict Form

After carefully reviewing all the materials, I have identified the following legal questions that a juror would need to answer. I organize them by claim, noting that the case has evolved significantly from the initial complaint through amendments and the judge's May 2025 order trimming certain claims.

---

## I. BREACH OF CONTRACT

**Question 1: Did a binding contract (the "Founding Agreement") exist between Elon Musk and the Defendants?**

*Why disputed:* Musk alleges that he, Altman, Brockman, and OpenAI entered into an agreement that OpenAI would (a) remain a nonprofit developing AGI for the benefit of humanity, not for private profit; and (b) operate on an open-source basis, subject only to safety considerations. Defendants contest that any enforceable contract existed — the communications cited (emails, the Certificate of Incorporation) may not constitute a binding agreement with definite terms, mutual assent, and consideration in the legal sense. The question of whether informal emails and a nonprofit's charter create enforceable contractual obligations to a donor is sharply contested.

**Question 2: If a contract existed, what were its material terms?**

*Why disputed:* Musk contends the agreement required OpenAI to remain a nonprofit, to open-source its technology, and to develop AGI for the public benefit. Defendants argue that Musk himself participated in discussions about converting to a for-profit structure in 2017, that Ilya Sutskever told Musk (with his agreement) that "Open" did not mean open-sourcing everything, and that the founding documents contemplated open-sourcing only "when applicable." The precise scope of any obligations is contested.

**Question 3: Did the Defendants breach the material terms of the Founding Agreement?**

*Why disputed:* Musk points to GPT-4 being kept closed-source, the exclusive licensing arrangement with Microsoft, and the transformation of OpenAI into what he calls a "de facto subsidiary" of Microsoft. Defendants respond that the evolution to a capped-profit structure was necessary to fulfill the mission, that Musk himself recognized the need for billions of dollars and even proposed merging with Tesla, and that OpenAI continues to serve its mission through broadly distributed AI tools.

**Question 4: Did Musk suffer damages as a proximate result of any breach, and if so, in what amount?**

*Why disputed:* Musk contributed approximately $44 million to OpenAI. The nature and amount of recoverable damages are contested — Musk's most recent amendment reportedly asks that any damages be directed to OpenAI's charity, which itself raises questions about the nature of his injury. Defendants may argue that Musk's donations were charitable gifts with no expectation of return, and that he suffered no cognizable legal injury.

---

## II. FRAUD / PROMISSORY ESTOPPEL

*(The judge's May 2025 order allowed fraud and unjust enrichment claims to proceed while dismissing some others.)*

**Question 5: Did Defendants make false representations or promises to Musk regarding OpenAI's mission, nonprofit structure, and/or open-source commitments?**

*Why disputed:* Musk alleges that Altman, Brockman, and OpenAI repeatedly promised him the organization would remain a nonprofit dedicated to open-source AGI for humanity's benefit. Defendants counter that Musk was a full participant in discussions about evolving the structure, that he was offered equity in the for-profit entity (which he declined), and that statements about the mission were aspirational rather than fraudulent.

**Question 6: At the time these representations were made, did Defendants intend to fulfill them, or did they intend to deceive Musk?**

*Why disputed:* Fraud requires a knowing misrepresentation or intent to deceive at the time of the statement. Defendants argue the representations were made in good faith and that circumstances changed (the need for massive capital), necessitating structural evolution. Musk argues the shift to a for-profit Microsoft partnership was a deliberate betrayal of promises made to induce his contributions.

**Question 7: Did Musk reasonably rely on Defendants' representations, and did he suffer detriment as a result of that reliance?**

*Why disputed:* Musk claims he donated over $44 million, recruited key talent, and provided other resources in reliance on these promises. Defendants point to evidence that Musk himself proposed a for-profit structure, sought majority control and the CEO title for a for-profit OpenAI, and created a Delaware public benefit corporation for this purpose in September 2017 — all of which could undermine a claim that he relied on promises that OpenAI would remain a pure nonprofit.

---

## III. UNJUST ENRICHMENT

**Question 8: Were the Defendants unjustly enriched by Musk's contributions, such that it would be inequitable for them to retain the benefits without compensating Musk?**

*Why disputed:* Musk argues his donations (intended for a nonprofit mission) were diverted to enrich Defendants and Microsoft. Defendants argue that Musk made voluntary charitable donations, received tax deductions for them, and that the contributions were used to advance the very research mission he supported. The question of whether retaining these benefits is "unjust" given the full history of the parties' interactions is contested.

---

## IV. ANTITRUST CLAIMS (Sherman Act § 1 and Clayton Act § 8)

**Question 9: Did Defendants engage in an unlawful interlocking directorate in violation of Section 8 of the Clayton Act?**

*Why disputed:* Musk alleges that Reid Hoffman simultaneously served on the boards of OpenAI and Microsoft (2017–2023), and that Deannah Templeton served as a Microsoft VP while sitting as a non-voting observer on OpenAI's board (2023–2024). Defendants argue that these arrangements have ended and are therefore moot. The FTC/DOJ Statement of Interest makes clear that voluntary cessation does not automatically moot such claims, and that "observer" status may still constitute an interlock depending on the facts — but the factual question of whether these arrangements actually created prohibited interlocks remains for the jury.

**Question 10: Did Defendants engage in an unlawful group boycott or concerted refusal to deal in violation of Section 1 of the Sherman Act?**

*Why disputed:* Musk alleges a "fund-no-competitors" edict whereby Microsoft and OpenAI coordinated to prevent investors from funding OpenAI's AI competitors. Defendants dispute that any such coordination occurred. The FTC/DOJ Statement of Interest clarifies that group boycotts can be per se unlawful even with vertical elements, but takes no position on whether the facts alleged here actually constitute such a boycott.

---

## V. UNFAIR BUSINESS PRACTICES (Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17200)

**Question 11: Did Defendants engage in unfair competition or unfair business practices by soliciting donations under false pretenses and/or by facilitating anticompetitive interlocking directorates?**

*Why disputed:* This claim overlaps substantially with the fraud, breach of contract, and antitrust claims. Musk argues that soliciting charitable donations while secretly planning to divert assets to a for-profit enterprise constitutes unfair business practices. Defendants argue that the structural evolution was transparent, publicly announced, and necessary. The UCL claim also potentially incorporates the interlocking directorate theory — the FTC notes that California courts look to Section 5 of the FTC Act for guidance on what constitutes "unfair" conduct under the UCL.

---

## VI. ACCOUNTING

**Question 12: Is Musk entitled to an accounting of the funds he and others donated to OpenAI, Inc. and of the intellectual property developed with those funds?**

*Why disputed:* Musk claims that because Defendants have diverted charitable contributions to for-profit purposes, he cannot ascertain the disposition of his contributions without an accounting. Defendants argue that Musk is a donor with no proprietary interest in OpenAI's assets or intellectual property, and that an accounting is unwarranted.

---

## VII. REMEDIES AND EQUITABLE RELIEF

**Question 13: If Defendants are found liable on any claim, what is the appropriate remedy?**

*Why disputed:* Musk has sought a range of remedies including: specific performance (requiring OpenAI to make its technology publicly available), injunctive relief (blocking the for-profit conversion and Microsoft's influence), restitution/disgorgement, and a judicial determination that GPT-4 (and successor models) constitute AGI and are therefore outside the scope of Microsoft's license. Musk's April 2026 amendment asks that any monetary damages go to OpenAI's charity and that Altman be removed from the board. Defendants contest virtually all of these remedies, and the question of whether a jury can or should order such equitable relief (versus the judge) is itself a legal question.

**Question 14: If Musk prevails on breach of contract, should the court order specific performance requiring Defendants to open-source their AGI technology and cease operating for Microsoft's private benefit?**

*Why disputed:* Specific performance is an extraordinary remedy. Musk argues it is necessary because monetary damages cannot adequately remedy the harm to humanity from a closed, for-profit AGI. Defendants argue that forced open-sourcing of advanced AI would create safety risks, that the court should not micromanage a technology company's operations, and that no contract entitles Musk to such relief.

---

## CROSSCUTTING FACTUAL QUESTIONS

**Question 15: Did Musk's own conduct — including his 2017 demand for majority equity, CEO title, and board control of a for-profit OpenAI, and his creation of the "Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc." public benefit corporation — waive, estop, or otherwise undermine his claims that Defendants breached an agreement to remain a nonprofit?**

*Why disputed:* This is perhaps the most hotly contested factual issue. Defendants have produced extensive documentary evidence (emails, iMessages, corporate filings) showing that Musk himself sought to convert OpenAI to a for-profit under his personal control. Musk argues that the discussions about a for-profit structure were premised on his maintaining control to protect the mission, and that when he could not achieve that, he reaffirmed support for the nonprofit — which Altman and Brockman also reaffirmed.

**Question 16: Does Musk's founding of xAI (a direct competitor to OpenAI, established March 2023) affect his standing, credibility, or the weight to be given to his claims?**

*Why disputed:* Defendants argue that Musk's lawsuit is motivated by competitive self-interest — that he seeks to hobble OpenAI to benefit his own AI company. Musk argues his motives are irrelevant to whether Defendants breached their obligations, and that the formation of xAI was a response to OpenAI's betrayal of its mission.

---

These questions capture the core factual and legal determinations a juror would need to make. Many of them are deeply intertwined — for example, the question of whether a binding contract existed is inseparable from the question of what the parties understood and intended during their 2015–2018 communications, and Musk's own 2017 conduct is relevant to virtually every claim.

transcript.user: Second task. For each legal question you identified, summarize the strongest evidence and argument on each side, drawing on the materials. Be specific about which document or fact supports which claim. Where the materials are silent or contested, say so. Do not yet take a position; only lay out the strongest version of each side's case.
transcript.assistant:
# Evidence and Arguments on Each Legal Question

---

## Question 1: Did a binding contract (the "Founding Agreement") exist?

### Strongest Case for Musk (Plaintiff):

**Documentary evidence of mutual promises and consideration:**

- The June 24, 2015 email from Altman to Musk laid out specific terms: "The mission would be to create the first general AI and use it for individual empowerment... The technology would be owned by the foundation and used 'for the good of the world.'" Musk replied: "Agree on all." (Complaint, ¶51, Ex. 2). This exchange has the hallmarks of offer and acceptance on specific terms.

- The December 8, 2015 Certificate of Incorporation formally memorialized these commitments: the corporation's "resulting technology will benefit the public and the corporation will seek to open source technology for the public benefit when applicable. The corporation is not organized for the private gain of any person." Property was "irrevocably dedicated" to these purposes. (Complaint, Ex. 1).

- The December 11, 2015 public announcement stated OpenAI's "research is free from financial obligations" and would "benefit humanity as a whole." (Complaint, Ex. 3).

- Consideration was substantial and clearly given in reliance: Musk contributed over $44 million from 2016–2020, recruited critical talent (including personally calling Ilya Sutskever on announcement day to secure him as Chief Scientist), leased office space through Musk Industries LLC, and provided ongoing strategic advice. (Complaint, ¶¶58–62).

- The agreement was reaffirmed multiple times. In April 2018, Altman sent Musk a draft Charter for feedback, describing the mission as ensuring AGI "benefits all of humanity" with the "primary fiduciary duty" being "to humanity." (Complaint, ¶66; OpenAI Charter). In March 2019, the OpenAI LP announcement warned investors that "it would be wise to view any investment in OpenAI LP in the spirit of a donation" and that the mission "take[s] precedence over any obligation to generate a profit." (Complaint, ¶67).

- The pattern of conduct — repeated written commitments, substantial consideration flowing in reliance on those commitments, and ongoing reaffirmation over years — is consistent with a binding contractual relationship, even if no single formal contract document was signed.

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

**No enforceable contract was formed:**

- The communications Musk cites are informal emails expressing shared aspirations, not a formal contract with defined obligations, remedies, and signatures. The June 2015 email exchange ("Agree on all") was a preliminary discussion about a project that had not yet been incorporated — it lacks the specificity and formality of a binding agreement.

- The Certificate of Incorporation is a corporate governance document filed with the State of Delaware — it creates obligations for the corporation to the state and public, not a bilateral contract with a specific donor. A nonprofit's articles of incorporation do not typically create enforceable contractual rights for individual donors.

- Musk's contributions were charitable donations to a 501(c)(3) organization, for which he presumably received tax deductions. Charitable donations generally do not create contractual relationships — they are gifts, not bargained-for exchanges. The consideration element is therefore lacking or at minimum contested.

- The terms Musk identifies are aspirational and vague. The Certificate says the corporation will "seek to open source technology for the public benefit when applicable" — the phrase "when applicable" is a significant qualifier that gives the organization discretion. "Benefit the public" and "for the good of the world" are mission statements, not enforceable contractual terms with definable breach conditions.

- No formal contract was ever drafted or signed between Musk individually and OpenAI specifying his rights, OpenAI's obligations, or remedies for breach. The materials contain no evidence of such a document.

---

## Question 2: If a contract existed, what were its material terms?

### Strongest Case for Musk:

**The terms were clear and specific:**

- **Nonprofit structure:** The Certificate of Incorporation states the corporation "is not organized for the private gain of any person" and its property is "irrevocably dedicated" to charitable purposes. (Complaint, Ex. 1). Altman himself said in September 2017: "I remain enthusiastic about the non-profit structure!" (openai_blog_for_profit, "Honest Thoughts" email chain).

- **Open-source commitment:** The Certificate states OpenAI "will seek to open source technology for the public benefit when applicable." The December 2015 announcement emphasized research "free from financial obligations." (Complaint, Ex. 3). OpenAI's early practice confirmed this understanding — GPT, GPT-2, and GPT-3 were all released with detailed papers and, in GPT-2's case, full open-source release. (Complaint, ¶¶79–83).

- **AGI for humanity, not private profit:** Altman's June 2015 email specified "The technology would be owned by the foundation and used 'for the good of the world.'" (Complaint, ¶51). The Charter states "Our primary fiduciary duty is to humanity." (openai_charter).

- **Safety as a countervailing consideration:** The only exception to openness was safety. Altman's original email stated "safety should be a first-class requirement." Ilya Sutskever told Musk that "as we get closer to building AI, it will make sense to start being less open" for safety reasons — and Musk agreed ("Yup"). (openai_blog_response). But this was about safety, not commercial secrecy.

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

**The terms were aspirational, qualified, and understood to be evolving:**

- The "open source" commitment was explicitly qualified: "when applicable." This gave OpenAI discretion about when and how to share technology.

- Musk himself agreed that openness was not absolute. When Sutskever wrote "The Open in openAI means that everyone should benefit from the fruits of AI after its built, but it's totally OK to not share the science," Musk replied "Yup." (openai_blog_response). This directly undermines the claim that the agreement required open-sourcing all technology.

- The understanding about organizational structure was explicitly acknowledged as evolving. In July 2017, Greg Brockman's message to Shivon Zilis recounted that Musk himself said the "non-profit was def the right one early on, may not be the right one now." (openai_blog_for_profit). Brockman agreed, writing that "the path must be: AI research non-profit (through end of 2017) → AI research + hardware for-profit (starting 2018)." Musk responded enthusiastically: "Let's talk Sat or Sun. I have a tentative game plan." (openai_blog_for_profit).

- The Charter itself, which Altman sent Musk for review, contemplated the need to "marshal substantial resources" — implicitly acknowledging the nonprofit model might not be sufficient. The OpenAI LP term sheet warned investors to treat investments "in the spirit of a donation," demonstrating that the for-profit subsidiary was designed to serve the mission, not abandon it.

- There was no "term" requiring that OpenAI remain a pure nonprofit in perpetuity. The entire trajectory of discussions from 2017 onward shows all parties — including Musk — recognizing the need for structural evolution.

---

## Question 3: Did Defendants breach the material terms?

### Strongest Case for Musk:

**GPT-4 as the paradigmatic breach:**

- GPT-4 was released in March 2023 with no scientific paper describing its architecture, training data, or internal design. (Complaint, ¶30). This was a radical departure from OpenAI's practice with GPT, GPT-2, and GPT-3, all of which were accompanied by detailed papers. The secrecy is "primarily driven by commercial considerations, not safety." (Complaint, ¶30).

- GPT-4 was exclusively licensed to Microsoft and integrated into Microsoft's commercial Office suite, making it "a de facto Microsoft proprietary algorithm." (Complaint, ¶30).

- Microsoft's own researchers wrote that GPT-4 "could reasonably be viewed as an early (yet still incomplete) version of an artificial general intelligence (AGI) system." (Complaint, ¶87, 89). If GPT-4 is AGI or near-AGI, it is expressly outside the scope of Microsoft's license — yet the board that determines AGI attainment was reconstituted with members hand-picked by Altman after the November 2023 board crisis. (Complaint, ¶¶31, 110).

- Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella boasted: "We have all the IP rights and all the capability. We have the people, we have the compute, we have the data, we have everything." (Complaint, ¶102). This confirms that OpenAI's technology has been effectively captured by a for-profit corporation.

- The November 2023 board crisis — where Altman was fired by a board concerned about his candor, then reinstated through Microsoft's leverage, with board members replaced by Altman-friendly picks — demonstrates that the nonprofit governance structure designed to protect humanity's interests has been captured by commercial interests. (Complaint, ¶¶94–111).

- OpenAI shuttered public access to previously available governing documents, financial statements, and conflict-of-interest rules. (Complaint, ¶118).

**The transformation is comprehensive:** OpenAI went from a nonprofit publishing open research for humanity to a closed-source, for-profit entity maximizing revenue for the world's largest corporation.

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

**OpenAI has pursued its mission at every step:**

- OpenAI provides broad access to its AI, including a free version of ChatGPT used by hundreds of millions of people. (openai_blog_response). Specific examples of public benefit include Albania using tools for EU accession, Digital Green helping farmers in Kenya and India, healthcare literacy improvements, and Icelandic language preservation. This is "broadly distributed benefits" — exactly what the Charter promises.

- The transition to a capped-profit structure was necessary to achieve the mission. Musk himself recognized this repeatedly:
  - December 2018: "Even raising several hundred million won't be enough. This needs billions per year immediately or forget it." (openai_blog_for_profit).
  - January 2018: "OpenAI is on a path of certain failure relative to Google." (openai_blog_for_profit).
  - Ilya Sutskever's July 2017 analysis showed AGI could require up to $10 billion in hardware alone, with exponential annual increases. (openai_blog_for_profit).

- Musk himself proposed merging OpenAI into Tesla — a for-profit corporation — and forwarded an email saying Tesla was "the only path that could even hope to hold a candle to Google." (openai_blog_response). If merging into Tesla was acceptable, the Microsoft partnership can hardly be characterized as a betrayal.

- The reduction in openness was anticipated and agreed to. Musk agreed with Sutskever that "it's totally OK to not share the science" as they got closer to AGI. (openai_blog_response). GPT-4's architecture not being published is consistent with this agreed-upon approach.

- The nonprofit board retains ultimate control. OpenAI GP, LLC (wholly owned by the nonprofit) remains the general partner. The Charter's commitment to humanity remains in force. The capped-profit structure limits investor returns. In October 2025, when OpenAI restructured further, the nonprofit received 26% ownership in the new entity. (wikipedia_case_summary).

---

## Question 4: Did Musk suffer damages, and if so, in what amount?

### Strongest Case for Musk:

- Musk contributed over $44 million to OpenAI between 2016 and September 2020. (Complaint, ¶61). These contributions were made in reliance on the Founding Agreement. If that agreement was breached, Musk is entitled to at minimum restitution of those funds.

- Beyond monetary damages, Musk invested substantial time and reputational capital — personally recruiting key talent, advising on research, leasing office space. The value of these non-monetary contributions is difficult to quantify but real.

- The broader harm is to the public (the intended third-party beneficiary), which has lost access to AGI technology that was supposed to be developed for humanity's benefit. Musk's April 2026 amendment asks that any monetary damages go to OpenAI's charity, underscoring that this is about the mission, not personal enrichment.

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

- Musk's contributions were tax-deductible charitable donations. He already received the tax benefit. Charitable donors do not typically have a right to restitution when they disagree with how a nonprofit evolves.

- Musk was offered equity in OpenAI LP on multiple occasions and declined. (openai_blog_for_profit, October 2022 iMessage exchange). He voluntarily chose not to participate in the for-profit upside.

- Musk has not identified a specific, quantifiable injury beyond his donations. His most recent amendment asking that damages go to charity suggests he cannot identify personal financial harm.

- Any harm to "humanity" or the "public" is speculative and not the type of concrete, particularized injury that supports a damages award to an individual plaintiff.

---

## Question 5: Did Defendants make false representations or promises?

### Strongest Case for Musk:

- Altman's June 2015 email explicitly promised: "The technology would be owned by the foundation and used 'for the good of the world.'" (Complaint, ¶51).

- The Certificate of Incorporation stated the corporation was "not organized for the private gain of any person" with property "irrevocably dedicated" to charitable purposes. (Complaint, Ex. 1).

- Altman said in September 2017: "I remain enthusiastic about the non-profit structure!" — this was a direct, written representation. (openai_blog_for_profit).

- The OpenAI Charter (sent to Musk for feedback in April 2018) stated: "Our primary fiduciary duty is to humanity." (openai_charter, Complaint ¶66).

- These representations continued through the 2019 OpenAI LP announcement, which told investors to treat their investment "in the spirit of a donation." (Complaint, ¶67).

- Despite all these promises, OpenAI has become a closed-source entity exclusively licensing its most powerful technology to Microsoft for commercial purposes.

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

- Every statement cited was made in good faith at the time. The evolution of OpenAI's structure was a response to changed circumstances (the massive capital requirements for AGI research) that all parties — including Musk — recognized.

- Altman's "I remain enthusiastic about the non-profit structure!" was said in the specific context of September 2017, when Musk's demand for majority control was rejected. It was a statement about the immediate path forward, not an eternal promise.

- The representations about the mission remain true: OpenAI's Charter still says the primary fiduciary duty is to humanity. The nonprofit still governs the for-profit subsidiary. The capped-profit structure limits returns. These are not false representations — they are representations that have been and continue to be honored.

- OpenAI has been transparent about its structural evolution: the March 2019 LP announcement, the Charter, and ongoing public communications all described the evolving structure. There was no concealment.

---

## Question 6: Did Defendants intend to deceive Musk at the time representations were made?

### Strongest Case for Musk:

- The pattern of behavior is suspicious: Altman and Brockman made repeated promises about the nonprofit mission while simultaneously exploring for-profit structures that would enrich them personally.

- The November 2023 board crisis is revealing. The board fired Altman for not being "consistently candid." (Complaint, ¶94). While the specific reasons remain undisclosed, this finding by Altman's own board supports the inference that Altman has a pattern of deception.

- Twelve former OpenAI employees filed an amicus brief in April 2025 stating that Altman "was a person of low integrity who had directly lied to employees." (wikipedia_case_summary). This is evidence of a pattern of dishonesty.

- The speed with which Altman moved to capture the board after being reinstated — replacing independent directors with allies, giving Microsoft an observer seat — suggests premeditation rather than good-faith adaptation to circumstances.

- The materials are largely silent on Altman's subjective intent at the time of the original founding promises, which is a gap that benefits Musk: intent to deceive can be inferred from the totality of conduct.

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

- There is no direct evidence that anyone intended to deceive Musk in 2015 when OpenAI was founded. The early emails show genuine enthusiasm for a nonprofit AI lab.

- The structural evolution took years to unfold and was driven by objective circumstances: the realization that AGI required billions of dollars per year. Ilya Sutskever's detailed July 2017 analysis explained the compute requirements with technical specificity. (openai_blog_for_profit). This was not a preconceived scheme.

- Musk was a full participant in the for-profit discussions. He was not deceived — he was part of the conversation. He wanted the for-profit conversion, just on his own terms.

- The board's statement about Altman not being "consistently candid" is ambiguous and relates to Altman's communications with the board in 2023, not to representations made to Musk in 2015–2018. It may not be admissible or relevant.

- Changed circumstances are not fraud. Promising to do something and later finding it impossible or impractical is not the same as making a promise you never intended to keep.

---

## Question 7: Did Musk reasonably rely on Defendants' representations, and suffer detriment?

### Strongest Case for Musk:

- Musk donated over $44 million explicitly because of the nonprofit mission. He would not have contributed to a for-profit venture designed to enrich its executives and Microsoft. The magnitude and duration of his contributions (2016–2020) demonstrate sustained reliance.

- Musk's recruitment efforts — including the critical call to secure Ilya Sutskever — were motivated by and premised on the nonprofit mission. He was "outmanned and outgunned" by Google and relied on "right on our side" as the differentiator. (Complaint, ¶58).

- His reliance was reasonable because the representations were made by the organization's co-founder and CEO, memorialized in corporate documents, and reaffirmed over multiple years.

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

- Musk's reliance is undermined by his own simultaneous conduct. In 2017, he:
  - Agreed that the nonprofit structure "may not be the right one now." (openai_blog_for_profit).
  - Proposed that OpenAI become "AI research + hardware for-profit" starting in 2018. (Brockman email, July 2017, which Musk endorsed).
  - Demanded majority equity (50–60%), board control, and the CEO title for a for-profit OpenAI. (openai_blog_for_profit, September 2017 emails and iMessages).
  - Directed his wealth manager to create "Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc." as a public benefit corporation. (openai_blog_for_profit, September 15, 2017 filing).

- A person who is simultaneously trying to convert the organization to a for-profit under his own control cannot reasonably claim to have relied on promises that it would remain a nonprofit.

- Musk continued donating after the March 2019 OpenAI LP announcement — meaning he was aware of the for-profit subsidiary and still chose to contribute. His reliance, to the extent it existed, was on a structure he knew was evolving.

- Musk was offered equity in OpenAI LP multiple times and declined. (openai_blog_for_profit, October 2022 texts). A person who declines the opportunity to participate in the upside has difficulty claiming detrimental reliance.

---

## Question 8: Were Defendants unjustly enriched?

### Strongest Case for Musk:

- Musk's $44 million in donations funded the early research (including work on GPT models and Dota 2) that became the foundation for OpenAI's commercially valuable technology. GPT-4, which is now monetized through Microsoft, is a direct derivative of research funded by Musk's charitable contributions.

- OpenAI's for-profit arm was valued at nearly $80 billion as of early 2024. (Complaint, ¶114). Altman and other insiders stand to benefit enormously from this valuation. The gap between $44 million in charitable donations and $80 billion in for-profit value represents staggering enrichment.

- It would be inequitable to allow Defendants to collect tax-exempt charitable donations under the pretense of a nonprofit mission, use those donations to develop technology, and then privatize the technology for personal gain. As Musk's complaint analogizes: "Imagine donating to a non-profit whose asserted mission is to protect the Amazon rainforest, but then the non-profit creates a for-profit Amazonian logging company." (Complaint, ¶122).

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

- Musk's total contributions were less than $45 million. OpenAI raised more than $90 million from other donors, and subsequently billions from Microsoft and other investors. (openai_blog_response). Musk's contributions, while significant early on, represent a small fraction of the total capital that built OpenAI's technology.

- The enrichment is not "unjust" because it was used for exactly the purpose Musk supported: advancing AGI research. The capped-profit structure was adopted specifically to attract the capital Musk himself said was necessary ("billions per year immediately or forget it").

- Musk received tax deductions for his charitable contributions — he already obtained a benefit. Allowing restitution on top of tax deductions would give him a double recovery.

- Musk was offered equity and declined. He cannot now claim unjust enrichment when he voluntarily chose not to participate in the value he helped create.

---

## Question 9: Did Defendants engage in unlawful interlocking directorates (Clayton Act § 8)?

### Strongest Case for Musk:

- Reid Hoffman simultaneously served on OpenAI's and Microsoft's boards from March 2017 to March 2023 — a period of six years during which Microsoft invested billions in OpenAI and the two entities negotiated exclusive licensing agreements. (FTC Statement, citing Am. Compl. ¶¶163, 371).

- Deannah Templeton simultaneously served as VP at Microsoft and as a non-voting member/observer on OpenAI's board from November 2023 to July 2024. (FTC Statement, citing Am. Compl. ¶¶168, 331(m), 374).

- The FTC/DOJ Statement of Interest powerfully supports Musk's legal theory:
  - Section 8 is per se unlawful — no proof of actual anticompetitive effect is required. (FTC Statement, citing TRW v. FTC, 647 F.2d at 946–47).
  - The interlock is not mooted by subsequent resignation. The defendant bears a "heavy burden" to show the conduct "could not reasonably be expected to recur." (FTC Statement, citing TRW at 953).
  - A board "observer" can be a "director" under Section 8 — companies cannot evade liability by calling their agents something other than directors. (FTC Statement, citing Square D, Reading Int'l).
  - The competitive harm from information exchange may persist even after the interlock ends. (FTC Statement at 5–6).

- OpenAI and Microsoft are competitors in AI — Microsoft operates its own AI research labs and products. The exclusive licensing arrangement does not eliminate the competitive relationship.

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

- Neither Hoffman nor Templeton currently serves on OpenAI's board. The interlocks have been unwound. (ECF No. 64 at 13, cited in FTC Statement).

- While the FTC states that voluntary cessation doesn't automatically moot a claim, the government "takes no position on any other issue in this case, including the facts alleged by Plaintiffs." (FTC Statement at 2). The FTC does not endorse Musk's specific factual allegations.

- Section 8 has specific jurisdictional thresholds (capital requirements, competitive sales minimums) that may not be met. The materials do not address whether these thresholds are satisfied.

- It is not clear that Microsoft and OpenAI were "competitors" within the meaning of Section 8 during the relevant period — they had a partnership/investment relationship. The question of whether they compete "such that the elimination of competition by agreement between them would constitute a violation of any of the antitrust laws" is a factual and legal question that is far from settled.

- The Hoffman interlock (2017–2023) and Templeton observer role (2023–2024) may have been understood as facilitating a cooperative relationship between investor and investee, not as anticompetitive coordination between rivals.

---

## Question 10: Did Defendants engage in an unlawful group boycott (Sherman Act § 1)?

### Strongest Case for Musk:

- Musk alleges a "fund-no-competitors" edict whereby Microsoft and/or OpenAI pressured investors not to fund competing AI companies. (FTC Statement at 10, referencing the PI motion).

- The FTC/DOJ Statement provides strong legal support:
  - Group boycotts can be per se unlawful, even with both horizontal and vertical elements. (FTC Statement, citing Honey Bum, Klor's).
  - Per se liability can attach when a vertical player (like a customer/investor) organizes a horizontal conspiracy among competitors. (FTC Statement, citing Apple, Toys "R" Us).
  - Concerted action can be inferred from conduct — no "formal agreement" is required. (FTC Statement at 10 n.6, citing Am. Tobacco, Twombly).
  - A boycott is unlawful even if the victim can find alternative resources. (FTC Statement, citing PLS.com, Klor's).

- The concentration of AI investment — with Microsoft as both OpenAI's primary backer and a dominant cloud computing provider — creates the structural conditions for such a boycott.

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

- The materials contain no specific evidence of a "fund-no-competitors" edict. The FTC Statement explicitly "takes no position on the alleged facts." (FTC Statement at 10).

- Investment decisions are ordinarily protected business judgment. An investor choosing not to fund a competitor of its portfolio company is rational, not conspiratorial.

- To establish a Section 1 violation, Musk must show concerted action — an agreement between two or more entities. Unilateral investment decisions by Microsoft, even if they disadvantage OpenAI's competitors, do not constitute a conspiracy.

- The AI investment landscape is highly competitive, with massive investments flowing from Google, Amazon, Apple, Meta, and others. The notion that Microsoft and OpenAI could orchestrate a boycott that meaningfully restricts funding for AI competitors is implausible given the breadth of available capital.

---

## Question 11: Did Defendants engage in unfair business practices under the UCL?

### Strongest Case for Musk:

- California's UCL reaches conduct that "threatens an incipient violation of an antitrust law, or violates the policy or spirit of one of those laws." (FTC Statement, citing Cel-Tech). The interlocking directorate arrangements and any fund-no-competitors edict violate the spirit of the Clayton and Sherman Acts.

- Soliciting charitable donations under the promise of a nonprofit mission, then converting those assets to for-profit use, constitutes unfair business practices under Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17510.8. (Complaint, ¶142).

- Even if the interlocking directorates don't technically violate Section 8 (e.g., because an observer is not technically a "director"), they violate the policy and spirit of the antitrust laws and are therefore actionable under the UCL and Section 5 of the FTC Act. The FTC Statement explicitly supports this theory: the FTC concluded in 1977 that interlocks "cannot escape liability through the allegedly porous wording of Section 8" and can be reached under the broader unfair competition standard. (FTC Statement at 8–9).

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

- The UCL claim is derivative of the underlying antitrust and fraud claims. If those claims fail on the facts, the UCL claim fails as well.

- OpenAI's structural evolution was publicly disclosed, transparent, and subject to ongoing public discussion. There was no deception of the type the UCL targets.

- The UCL prohibits "unfair" competition — but competition in the AI industry is vigorous and thriving. Multiple well-funded competitors (Google, Meta, Anthropic, xAI itself) are active in the market. There is no evidence of actual competitive harm.

---

## Question 12: Is Musk entitled to an accounting?

### Strongest Case for Musk:

- Musk donated $44 million to a nonprofit with specific stated purposes. He has a right to know how those funds — and the intellectual property they funded — have been used, especially given allegations that they were diverted to for-profit purposes. (Complaint, ¶¶146–148).

- OpenAI has made it harder to access this information: a WIRED investigation found that OpenAI changed its policy and no longer provides governing documents, financial statements, and conflict-of-interest rules to the public, despite long representing that it would. (Complaint, ¶118).

- The complexity of OpenAI's corporate structure — with at least eight identified entities across multiple LLCs, LPs, and holding companies — makes it impossible for Musk to trace his contributions without an accounting. (Complaint, ¶¶70–76).

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

- A donor to a 501(c)(3) does not have a proprietary interest in the organization's assets. IRS Form 990 filings provide the public accountability that the law requires for nonprofits.

- An accounting is an equitable remedy that requires Musk to establish that he has an ownership or quasi-ownership interest in the assets at issue. As a donor, he has no such interest — his donations became the property of OpenAI, Inc. upon receipt.

- The appropriate oversight mechanism for nonprofit conduct is the state Attorney General, not a private donor. Notably, the California Attorney General declined to join Musk's lawsuit in April 2025. (wikipedia_case_summary).

---

## Question 13: What is the appropriate remedy if Defendants are found liable?

### Strongest Case for Musk:

- Specific performance is warranted because monetary damages cannot adequately address the harm. The Founding Agreement was about ensuring AGI benefits all of humanity — money cannot substitute for that. Musk seeks an order requiring OpenAI to make its technology publicly available and prohibiting it from operating for Microsoft's private benefit. (Complaint, Prayer for Relief A).

- Musk also seeks a judicial determination that GPT-4 and successor models constitute AGI and are therefore outside the scope of Microsoft's license. (Complaint, Prayer for Relief B, C).

- His April 2026 amendment — asking that damages go to OpenAI's charity and that Altman be removed from the board — demonstrates that this is about the mission, not personal enrichment, which supports equitable relief.

- Disgorgement of profits is appropriate to prevent Defendants from benefiting from their breach.

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

- Specific performance is an extraordinary remedy rarely granted, and courts do not typically order ongoing supervision of a technology company's business operations.

- A judicial determination that GPT-4 is AGI would require the court to make a technical finding that even AI researchers disagree about. The Microsoft researchers' paper about "sparks of AGI" was explicitly tentative ("early yet still incomplete version").

- Removing a CEO (Altman) from a private company's board is an extraordinary intrusion into corporate governance that courts are reluctant to order.

- Musk's own competing interests (as founder of xAI) create serious concerns about the remedies he seeks — an order forcing OpenAI to open-source its technology would directly benefit his competitor.

---

## Question 14: Should the court order specific performance requiring open-sourcing?

### Strongest Case for Musk:

- The Founding Agreement specifically contemplated open-source technology. The Certificate of Incorporation says OpenAI "will seek to open source technology for the public benefit when applicable." Forcing compliance with this commitment is not extraordinary — it is simply enforcement of the agreed terms.

- OpenAI's own early practice demonstrated that open-sourcing was feasible: GPT, GPT-2, and GPT-3 were all released with detailed papers or full source code.

- The harm from keeping AGI closed is irreversible. If AGI technology is locked away in a for-profit corporation, the foundational purpose of OpenAI is permanently defeated. Only specific performance can address this.

### Strongest Case for Defendants:

- The "when applicable" qualifier gives OpenAI discretion. Musk himself agreed that "it's totally OK to not share the science" near AGI. (openai_blog_response). Forcing open-sourcing could violate the safety considerations that both parties acknowledged.

- Open-sourcing advanced AI models could pose genuine safety risks — a concern recognized by AI researchers, policymakers, and even Musk himself (who co-signed the March 2023 letter calling for a pause in training systems more powerful than GPT-4).

- Specific performance would require ongoing judicial supervision of highly technical decisions about what to release, when, and how — something courts are poorly equipped to do.

- Musk operates xAI, a direct competitor. Ordering OpenAI to open-source its technology would directly benefit Musk's commercial competitor, creating a profound conflict of interest.

---

## Question 15: Did Musk's own conduct waive or undermine his claims?

### Strongest Case for Defendants (as this question primarily benefits them):

The evidence of Musk's own for-profit ambitions is extensive and well-documented:

- **Musk questioned the nonprofit structure from the start.** On November 20, 2015 — before OpenAI was even publicly announced — Musk wrote: "the structure doesn't seem optimal... Probably better to have a standard C corp with a parallel nonprofit." (openai_blog_for_profit).

- **Musk agreed the nonprofit structure needed to change.** In July 2017, Brockman reported
