PRAYER FOR RELIEF 
WHEREFORE, Plaintiff prays for judgment against Defendants as follows: 
A. For an order compelling specific performance of Defendants’ repeated contractual 
promises including, without limitation,  
i. An order requiring that Defendants continue to follow OpenAI’s long-
standing practice of making AI research and technology developed at 
OpenAI available to the public, and 
ii. An order prohibiting Defendants from utilizing OpenAI, Inc. or its assets 
for the financial benefit of the individual Defendants, Microsoft, or any 
other particular person or entity; 
B. For a judicial determination that GPT-4 constitutes Artificial General Intelligence 
and is thereby outside the scope of OpenAI’s license to Microsoft; 
C. For a judicial determination that Q* and/or other OpenAI next generation large 
language models in development constitute(s) Artificial General Intelligence and 
is/are outside the scope of OpenAI’s license to Microsoft; 
D. Injunctive relief consistent with and to effectuate items A through C above; 
E. For an award of restitution and/or disgorgement of any and all monies received by 
Defendants while they engaged in the unfair and improper practices described 
herein, in addition to prejudgment interest and penalties, pursuant to Business & 
Professions Code § 17200 et seq.; 
F. For an accounting of funds donated by Plaintiffs and others to OpenAI, Inc., and of 
intellectual property or derivative works funded by same, and of Defendants’ use 
of the same for personal benefit or the benefit of any individual or third-party entity; 
G. For general, compensatory, and punitive damages to be proven at trial, each of 
which Plaintiff will contribute to a non-profit or charity; 
H. For attorneys’ fees pursuant to California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021.5; 
I. For pre-judgment and post-judgment interest at the maximum legal rate; and 
J. For such other and further relief as the Court may deem just and proper.

INTRODUCTION 
The United States, through the U.S. Department of Justice, joined by the Federal Trade 
Commission (“FTC”), respectfully submits this Statement of Interest pursuant to 28 U.S.C. § 
517. The Antitrust Division of the U.S. Department of Justice and the FTC (collectively, the 
“Agencies”) enforce the federal antitrust laws, including the prohibition against interlocking 
directorates in Section 8 of the Clayton Act, 15 U.S.C. § 19,
1 and the prohibition against 
contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade in Section 1 of the Sherman Act, 
15 U.S.C. § 1. The Agencies therefore have a strong interest in their correct application.   
The FTC is further charged by Congress with preventing unfair methods of competition 
under Section 5 of the FTC Act, 15 U.S.C § 45.  Such unfair methods include “incipient 
violations of [the Clayton Act], and conduct which, although not a violation of the letter of the 
antitrust laws, is close to a violation or is contrary to their spirit.”  E.I. du Pont de Nemours & 
Co. v. FTC (Ethyl), 729 F.2d 128, 136–37 (2d Cir. 1984). Califo rnia similarly prohibits unfair 
competition, Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17200, and federal and state courts look to Section 5 of 
the FTC Act to interpret that statute’s meaning. See Cel-Tech Commc’ns, Inc. v. Los Angeles 
Cellular Tel. Co., 20 Cal. 4th 163, 185–86 (1999) (“As the issue before us in this case arises out 
of a claim of unfair competition . . . the relevant jurisprudence would be that arising under 
section 5’s prohibition against ‘unfair methods of competition.’”).  The FTC therefore has an 
interest in the correct interpretation of California’s Unfair Competition Law (“UCL”), Cal. Bus. 
& Prof. Code § 17200. 
The Agencies file this Statement of Interest in response to Plaintiffs’ Motion for a 
Preliminary Injunction, ECF No. 46, to address the proper standards for analyzing whether (1) a

FACTUAL BACKGROUND 
The First Amended Complaint, ECF No. 32, alleges that “Artificial General 
Intelligence,” i.e., “a machine having intelligence for a wide variety of tasks like a human,” 
poses certain risks. Am. Compl. ¶¶ 4, 74-75. To  address these risks, Plaintiff Musk helped 
establish OpenAI in 2015. Am. Compl. ¶ 88. That same year, OpenAI incorporated as a 
“nonprofit corporation” committed to the “research, development and distribution of technology 
related to artificial intelligence.”  Am. Compl. ¶ 89.   
The First Amended Complaint further alleges that (1) defendant Reid Hoffman 
simultaneously served on OpenAI’s and Microsoft’s boards of directors from March 2017 to 
March 2023, and (2) defendant Deannah Templeton simultaneously served as Vice President of 
Partnerships and Operations in the Technology & Research division at Microsoft and as a non-
voting member of OpenAI’s board of directors from November 2023 until July 2024.  Am. 
Compl. ¶¶ 163, 371; see also id. ¶¶ 168, 331(m), 374. 
Based on these allegations, and others, Plaintiffs bring 26 separate claims including 
breach of contract, fraud, false advertising, breach of fiduciary duty, and claims under the 
Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act.  Plaintiffs also allege Defendants violated 
the Sherman and Clayton Acts based in part on conduct affecting the market for “generative AI 
models and platforms.” Am. Compl. ¶ 204. 
On November 29, 2024, Plaintiffs filed a Motion for a Preliminary Injunction seeking to 
enjoin Defendants from, among other things, “directly or indirectly undertaking any action for 
the purpose of or tending to have the effect of, interlocking directorates or benefitting from

ARGUMENT 
The Agencies offer their views on the proper application of Section 8 of the Clayton Act, 
Section 1 of the Sherman Act, and the UCL to the specific issues discussed below.  The 
Agencies take no position on the ultimate disposition of the motion. 
I. Section 8’s Bar on Interlocking Directorates 
A. Statutory Background 
Section 8 of the Clayton Act provides that “[n]o person shall, at the same time, serve as a 
director or officer in any two corporations . . . that are . . . competitors such that the elimination 
of competition by agreement between them would constitute a violation of any of the antitrust 
laws.” 15 U.S.C. § 19. Section 8 makes interloc king directorates, i.e., overlapping directors or 
officers among competing companies, per se unlawful.  TRW, Inc. v. FTC, 647 F.2d 942, 948 
(9th Cir. 1981). At the same time, this broad prohibition is subject to certain limitations and 
exceptions. See, e.g., 15 U.S.C. § 19(a)(2), (b).  For example, the companies must be engaged in 
interstate commerce and have “capital, surplus, and undivided profits aggregating more than” 
$48,559,000. Id.  Section 8 also does not apply if competitive sales: (A) of either corporation are 
less than $4,855,900; (B) of either corporation are less than two percent of the corporation’s total 
sales; or (C) of each corporation are less than four percent of the corporation’s total sales.  Id. at 
§ 8(a)(2).  Finally, Section 19(b) grants a one-year grace period to individuals who were eligible 
to serve at the time of election but a change in circumstances later created an illegal interlock.  
The purpose of Section 8’s prohibition on interlocking directorates is “to nip in the bud 
incipient antitrust violations by removing the opportunity or temptation for such violations.”  
TRW, 647 F.2d at, 946–47.  Put another way, Section 8 “prevent[s] restraints on competition 
before they materialize[] by outlawing a particular practice thought to facilitate such restraints.”  
Id. at 948; see also Reading Int’l, Inc. v. Oaktree Cap. Mgmt. LLC, 317 F. Supp. 2d 301, 330 
(S.D.N.Y. 2003). For example, preventing interl ocking directorates helps “avoid the opportunity

Case 4:24-cv-04722-YGR Document 87 Filed 01/10/25 Page 9 of 17 
for the coordination of business decisions by competitors and . . . the exchange of commercially 
sensitive information by competitors.” Square D Co. v. Schneider S.A., 760 F. Supp. 362, 366 
(S.D.N.Y. 1991). Such conduct can facilitate unr easonable restraints of trade in violation of 
Section 1 of the Sherman Act (15 U.S.C. § 1) or help a monopolist maintain and entrench its 
power in violation of Section 2 of the Sherman Act (15 U.S.C. § 2).  In this way, Section 8 
supports and reinforces the federal antitrust laws more generally.  See United States v. Sears, 
Roebuck & Co., 111 F. Supp. 614, 616 (S.D.N.Y. 1953) (“Section 8 was but one of a series of 
measures which finally emerged as the Clayton Act, all intended to strengthen the Sherman 
Act.”). 
Courts have consistently interpreted Section 8 in view of these “prophylactic and 
remedial purposes.” Square D Co. v. Schneider S.A., 760 F. Supp. 362, 366 (S.D.N.Y. 1991). 
For example, the Ninth Circuit has held that establishing a Section 8 violation does not require 
“proof that the interlock has an actual anticompetitive effect.” TRW, 647 F.2d at 946–47 (citing 
Protectoseal Co. v. Barancik, 484 F.2d 585, 589 (7th Cir. 1973) (Stevens, J.)). “On the contrary, 
the statute reflects a public interest in preventing directors from serving in positions which 
involve either a potential conflict of interest or a potential frustration of competition.”  
Protectoseal, 484 F.2d at 589. Likewise, courts have refuse d to read the statute so rigidly that a 
company can evade Section 8 liability through tactics that elevate form over substance, e.g., “by 
calling its agents on the competitors’ board something other than either officers or directors.”  
Square, 760 F. Supp. at 366; Reading, 317 F. Supp. 2d at 327–28 (rejecting reading of Section 8 
that would “elevate form over substance” and render the statue “a formalism”). 
B. Section 8 Claims Are Not Mooted by Simply Unwinding an Interlock  
Defendants argue “there is no live ‘case or controversy’” here because “neither Hoffman 
nor Templeton is currently affiliated with OpenAI’s board in any capacity.”  ECF No. 64 at 13. 
But ending an interlocking directorate, e.g., by having a person resign from a corporate board, is 
not sufficient, on its own, to moot a claim under Section 8 of the Clayton Act.  See United States 
v. W. T. Grant Co., 345 U.S. 629, 632 (1953); TRW, 647 F.2d at 953. In resolving this matter, 
the Court should avoid holding otherwise.

Case 4:24-cv-04722-YGR Document 87 Filed 01/10/25 Page 10 of 17 
“[A]s a general rule, ‘voluntary cessation of allegedly illegal conduct does not deprive the 
tribunal of power to hear and determine the case, i.e., does not make the case moot.’”  Cty. of Los 
Angeles v. Davis, 440 U.S. 625, 631 (1979). Otherwise “[a] defendant [would be] free to return 
to his old ways,” and that, “together with a public interest in having the legality of the [disputed] 
practices settled, militates against a mootness conclusion.”  United States v. W. T. Grant Co., 345 
U.S. 629, 632 (1953). Thus, where a defendant volunt arily ceases illegal conduct, it still bears a 
“heavy burden” to demonstrate it is “absolutely clear that the allegedly wrongful behavior could 
not reasonably be expected to recur.” TRW, 647 F.2d at 953 (quoting United States v. 
Concentrated Phosphate Export Ass’n, 393 U.S. 199, 203 (1968)). Additionally, the relevant 
“concern is with repeated violations of the same law, and not merely with repetition of the same 
offensive conduct.” Id. 
The issue of mootness in the context of Section 8 was addressed squarely by the Ninth 
Circuit in TRW. 647 F.2d at 953. There, the interlocking director (i) resigned his position at the 
competing company, (ii) “decided not to stand for reelection,” (iii) and “provided sworn 
assurances that [he] would avoid offensive directorships in the future.”  Id. The Ninth Circuit 
still held the case was not moot.  Id. As the Ninth Circuit expl ained, mootness depends on the 
risk that a defendant may violate Section 8 in the future, not simply whether it might resume the 
specific interlock at issue.  Id. That risk may remain even af ter an interlock is unwound despite 
“good faith . . . assurances of future compliance.” Id. 
Moreover, “a potential conflict of interest or a potential frustration of competition,” 
Protectoseal, 484 F.2d at 589, may remain even after an interlock is resolved and even if one is 
unlikely to recur. For example, if compan ies use interlocking directors to exchange 
competitively sensitive information, see Square, 760 F. Supp. at 367, they may frustrate 
competition so long as the information remains sensitive and in their possession.  Thus, holding 
that a company moots a potential claim under Section 8 by simply ending an interlock would be 
contrary to the purpose of the statute and would significantly undermine enforcement of the 
Clayton Act. Cf. United States v. Paramount Pictures, 334 U.S. 131, 171–72 (1948) (“[T]he 
requirement that the defendants restore what they unlawfully obtained [through an unlawful

antitrust conspiracy] is no more punishment than the familiar remedy of restitution.”); United 
Auto Workers Advisory Opinion, 97 F.T.C. 933 (May 1, 1981) (“The basic purposes of Section 8 
are to avoid the opportunity for coordination of business decisions by competitors and to prevent 
the exchange of commercially sensitive information among competitors.”) (emphasis added). 
Unwound interlocks are subject to the same mootness analysis as other abandoned per se conduct 
such as market allocation or price fixing.  W. T. Grant Co., 345 U.S. at 632 (voluntary cessation 
of Section 8 interlock does not deprive the tribunal of power to hear and determine the case); 
United States v. Concentrated Phosphate Exp. Ass’n, 393 U.S. 199, 203 (1968) (“mere voluntary 
cessation of allegedly illegal conduct does not moot a case” involving price-fixing under Section 
1); United States v. Socony-Vacuum Oil Co., 310 U.S. 150, 224 n.59 (1940) (“It is the ‘contract, 
combination . . . or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce’ which § 1 of the Act strikes 
down, whether the concerted activity be wholly nascent or abortive on the one hand, or 
successful on the other.”) (citation omitted). 
Accordingly, if a plaintiff properly pleads a likelihood of recurrence or an ongoing harm 
through the wrongful retention of competitively sensitive information obtained through the 
alleged interlocks, Section 8 claims are not moot.  See TRW, 647 F.2d at 953. Even in the face of 
good faith assurances of future compliance with Section 8, a court may find that “the heavy 
burden of proof regarding mootness has not been satisfied.”  Id. 
C. Section 8 Bars Relationships that Create an Interlock Regardless of Form  
It is well-established that an individual or a company can violate Section 8 when it 
“serve[s] as a director or officer” directly or indirectly, e.g., through an agent, deputy, or 
representative. For example, in TRW, the Ninth Circuit held that “section 8 prohibits 
corporations from choosing, and natural persons from serving as, directors in violation of section 
8’s substantive requirements.” Id. at 949. Consistent with TRW, if a company chooses a natural 
person to serve on a competitor’s board as its agent, the company and the natural person violate 
Section 8 unless an exception or safe harbor applies.

corporations (and individuals) to evade antitrust liability simply by designating agents to serve 
their bidding on the boards of competing businesses.”  Reading, 317 F. Supp. 2d at 331; see also 
California v. Fed. Power Comm’n, 369 U.S. 482, 485 (1962) (“Immunity from the antitrust laws 
is not lightly implied.”). Cons equently, an individual cannot evade liability by serving as an 
“observer” on a competitor’s board.  Similarly, companies cannot evade liability by relying on an 
agent to serve on a competitor’s board. In resolving this matter, the Court should avoid holding 
otherwise. 
A board “observer” may be a “director” under 15 U.S.C. § 19 in certain circumstances.  
Specifically, a company or individual cannot use an indirect means to a prohibited end, such as 
by asking another person to serve as a board observer to obtain entry to a meeting that is 
otherwise off limits due to Section 8’s ban on interlocks.  Such misdirection would undermine 
Section 8’s intent to impose a clear ban on direct involvement in the management of a 
competitor. Accordingly, the Court should determine whether Defendants violated Section 8 
based on case-specific allegations without “exalt[ing] form over substance,” Square, 760 F. 
Supp. at 366, or disturbing longstanding precedent holding that a company may violate Section 8 
when it appoints an agent, deputy, or representative to serve on the board of a competitor, see 
TRW, 647 F.2d at 949; see also Square, 760 F. Supp. at 367 (“[A] cause of action under § 8 is 
stated where a company attempts to place on the Board of a competitor individuals who are 
agents of, and have an employment or business relationship with, such company.”); Reading, 
317 F. Supp. 2d at 331-32 (similar). 
II. Interlocking Directorates Are an Unfair Method of Competition 
Interlocking directorates are an unfair method of competition under federal antitrust law, 
including the FTC Act.  Courts have looked to Section 5 of the FTC Act and its prohibition 
Ohio 1974), aff’d, 513 F.2d 633 (6th Cir. 1975).  In each case, however, the key question is 
whether “it can legitimately be said that it is [the defendant] as an entity . . . which ‘serve[s] as a 
director’ of [the competing company].” Reading, 317 F. Supp. 2d at 331–32; see also Blau v. 
Lehman, 368 U.S. 403, 410 (1962) (“Lehman Brothers would be a ‘director’ of Tide Water [for 
purposes of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934], if . . . Lehman actually functioned as a 
director through Thomas, who had been deputized by Lehman to perform a director’s duties not 
for himself but for Lehman.”).

against unfair methods of competition for guidance on how to interpret California’s UCL.  See, 
e.g., Cel-Tech Commc’ns, 20 Cal. 4th at 185 (citations omitted) (“‘In view of the similarity of 
language and obvious identity of purpose of the two statutes, decisions of the federal court on the 
subject are more than ordinarily persuasive.’”); Epic Games v. Apple, 67 F.4th 946, 1000–1002 
(9th Cir. 2023) (discussing the relationship between state unfair competition claims and federal 
antitrust claims). “The standard of ‘unfairness’ under the FTC Act is, by necessity, an elusive 
one, encompassing not only practices that violate the Sherman Act and the other antitrust laws, 
but also practices that the Commission determines are against public policy for other reasons.”  
Fed. Trade Comm’n v. Amazon.com, Inc., No. 2:23-cv-01495-JHC, 2024 WL 4448815, at *13 
(W.D. Wash. Sept. 30, 2024) (quoting Fed. Trade Comm’n v. Indiana Fed’n of Dentists, 476 
U.S. 447, 454 (1986)). See also Policy Statement Regarding the Scope of Unfair Methods of 
Competition Under Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act, File No. P221202, at 1 
(F.T.C. Nov. 10, 2022) (“Section 5 reaches beyond the Sherman and Clayton Acts to encompass 
various types of unfair conduct that tend to negatively affect competitive conditions.”) 
(collecting cases). 
The Supreme Court has stated that the FTC Act’s prohibition on unfair methods of 
competition was “designed to supplement and bolster the Sherman Act and the Clayton Act . . . 
to stop in their incipiency acts and practices which, when full blown, would violate [the Sherman 
Act and the Clayton Act] . . . .” Fed. Trade Comm’n v. Motion Picture Advertising Service Co., 
344 U.S. 392, 394–95 (1953) (internal citations omitted); see also Ethyl, 729 F.2d at 136–37 (the 
FTC Act empowers the FTC “to bar incipient violations of those statutes, and conduct which, 
although not a violation of the letter of the antitrust laws, is close to a violation or is contrary to 
their spirit”) (cleaned up); Fed. Trade Comm’n v. Texaco, Inc., 393 U.S. 223, 225 (1968) (same). 
The Supreme Court has likewise long recognized that the FTC “has broad powers to

III. Group Boycotts Under Section 1 
Plaintiffs seek to enjoin an alleged “fund-no-competitors” edict, which they unify under a 
group boycott theory of antitrust, in violation of Section 1 of the Sherman Act.  The Agencies 
take no position on the alleged facts but file this statement in order to clarify the proper legal 
analysis for group boycotts.
5  Section 1 of the Sherman Act forbids “[e]very contract, 
combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade.” 15 U.S.C. § 1.  
A claim under Section 1 therefore requires two key elements: (1) “a contract, combination, or 
conspiracy”—i.e., “concerted action”— (2) that “unreasonably restrains trade.”  Am. Needle, Inc. 
v. Nat’l Football League, 560 U.S. 183, 186 (2010). Concerted action can be unreasonable in 
one of two ways. Ohio v. Am. Express Co., 585 U.S. 529, 540 (2018). First, it may be 
unreasonable per se, without any further inquiry into its competitive effects or justifications, 
because of its inherently anticompetitive “nature and character.”  Standard Oil Co. of New Jersey 
v. United States, 221 U.S. 1, 64 (1911). Typically, only ho rizontal restraints, i.e., concerted 
action between actual or potential competitors, are per se unlawful.  Am. Express, 585 U.S. at 
541. Second, a restraint may be unreasonable under the “rule of reason,” a “fact-specific 
assessment” of challenged conduct’s “effect on competition.”  Id. (internal quotation marks 
omitted). Group boycotts involving competing firm s can properly be analyzed under the per se 
rule. See Honey Bum, LLC v. Fashion Nova, Inc., 63 F.4th 813, 820–21 (9th Cir. 2023). 
 The litigants disagree about whether the alleged fund-no-competitors edict is horizontal 
or vertical.6  This distinction does not preclude application of Section 1.  Without commenting

coordinated conduct under the Sherman Act or, alternatively, may be characterized as “an 
unregulated merger.” The charac terization of the relationship between the entities for antitrust 
purposes is a factual inquiry on which the Agencies take no position at this time. 
6 To the extent Defendants dispute the existence of concerted action, i.e., a group boycott against 
OpenAI’s rivals, the Court would err if it required Plaintiffs to establish the existence of a

on the facts as alleged, a group boycott can have a vertical aspect; and per se liability under 
Section 1 can attach so long as there is also a horizontal element to the alleged conspiracy.  See 
NYNEX Corp. v. Discon, Inc., 525 U.S. 128, 136 (1998) (surveying precedent and distinguishing 
purely vertical agreement between supplier and customer from cases also involving a horizontal 
agreement among competitors). Indeed, “[t]he horizontal agreement can exist either among the 
initiators of the boycott (as in Fashion Originators) or those pressured into joining (as in 
Klor’s).” Honey Bum, 63 F.4th at 821. Thus, a conspiracy  among competing firms can be per se 
unlawful even if it is organized by one of their customers, as is the case with some hub-and-
spoke conspiracies. E.g., Klor’s Inc. v. Broadway-Hale Stores, Inc., 359 U.S. 207, 211 (1959); 
Toys “R” Us, Inc. v. FTC, 221 F.3d 928, 936–38 (7th Cir. 2000); United States v. Apple, Inc., 
791 F.3d 290, 323 (2d Cir. 2015). For example, in Apple, a price fixing conspiracy was 
condemned as per se unlawful even though it was organized by a distributor (Apple) that did not 
compete with the other co-conspirators (book publishers).  791 F.3d at 323 (2d Cir. 2015) 
(anticompetitive “threat is just as significant when a vertical market participant organizes the 
conspiracy”). 
Moreover, group boycotts are “not to be tolerated merely because the victim is just one 
merchant whose business is so small that his destruction makes little difference to the economy.” 
Klor’s, 359 U.S. at 213 (“Monopoly can as surely thrive by the elimination of such small 
businessmen, one at a time, as it can by driving them out in large groups”).  And the fact that 
alternative resources may be secured by the boycotted firm beyond those supplied by the 
boycotting group cannot serve as a defense. See PLS.com, 32 F.4th at 835 (“[A] group of 
competitors coercing a competitor’s suppliers . . . constitutes a group boycott even if the 
competitors do not completely cut off the competitor’s access to inputs it needs.”); Klor’s, 359 
“formal agreement” between members of the alleged boycott.  Am. Tobacco v. United States, 328 
U.S. 781, 809 (1946). A “tacit” agreement can also qualify.  Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 
U.S. 544, 553 (2007); see White v. R.M. Packer Co., 635 F.3d 571, 576 (1st Cir. 2011) (A tacit 
agreement is “one in which only the conspirators’ actions . . . indicate [its] existence.”).  For 
example, concerted action may be inferred if one firm invites others to participate in a boycott 
and then those firms engage in conduct showing acceptance.  PLS.com v. Nat’l Ass’n of Realtors, 
32 F.4th 824, 843 (9th Cir. 2022) (citing Interstate Cir. v. United States, 306 U.S. 208, 227 
(1939)).

(
April 2026
)
Musk v. Altman
is an ongoing lawsuit in the
United States District Court for the Northern District of California
in which
Elon Musk
accuses
OpenAI
and its executives, including CEO
Sam Altman
, of violating the company's founding agreement by prioritizing profits over AI safety.
Background
[
edit
]
This section
needs expansion
. You can help by
adding missing information
.
(
April 2026
)
Musk and Altman are co-founders of OpenAI, which was founded in 2015. Musk left in 2018 in the aftermath of a
power struggle
.
[
1
]
Timeline
[
edit
]
Leadup (2024–2026)
[
edit
]
The lawsuit was originally filed on February 29, 2024.
[
2
]
[
3
]
[
4
]
[
5
]
In November 2024, Musk filed a motion for
preliminary injunction
to block OpenAI from changing from a nonprofit to a for-profit.
He believes the conversion violates the terms of his contributions, which total $44 million, to OpenAI from 2016 to 2020.
[
6
]
In February 2025, United States District Judge
Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers
said that Musk's claim of irreparable harm was a stretch.
[
7
]
In April 2025, twelve former employees of OpenAI stated in an amicus brief that OpenAI had abandoned its nonprofit roots and that Altman "was a person of low integrity who had directly lied to employees about the extent of his knowledge and involvement in OpenAI’s practices of forcing departing employees to sign lifetime non-disparagement agreements." The motion was filed by Harvard law professor
Lawrence Lessig
.
[
8
]
In April 2025, OpenAI countersued Musk, claiming that his actions were deliberate tactics to slow OpenAI in order to benefit his own interests.
[
9
]
That month, the California attorney general declined to join Musk's lawsuit.
[
10
]
On May 1, 2025, Judge
Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers
trimmed the lawsuit, excluding claims of false advertising and breach of fiduciary duty, but allowed claims of fraud and unjust enrichment to proceed.
[
11
]
On May 5, 2025, OpenAI announced that it was no longer planning to restructure into a for-profit company separate from its nonprofit board. Later that day, Musk's lawyer
Marc Toberoff
, announced that the lawsuit against OpenAI would continue.
[
12
]
In October 2025, OpenAI formed a for-profit company –  OpenAI Group PBC – with the original non-profit receiving 26 percent ownership in the new entity and Microsoft receiving a 27 percent stake.
[
13
]
As part of the case files disclosed by Musk's lawyers in federal court in November 2025, Altman was shown to have reached out to
Shivon Zilis
on February 9, 2023, asking her advice about whether to publicly praise Musk on X, which he later did.
[
14
]
In April 2026, Musk amended the lawsuit asking that any monetary damages he might be awarded be given to OpenAI's charity and that Altman be removed from OpenAI's board.
[
15
]
In addition to Altman, various other OpenAI executives, including OpenAI president
Greg Brockman
, are being sued by Musk in the lawsuit as well.
[
16
]
[
17
]

edit

for the case began on April 27, 2026.
[
16
]
[
17
]
On April 28, 2026, Elon Musk began his testimony against Altman and OpenAI, lasting until April 30;
[
18
]
[
19
]
he was also questioned by both OpenAI's attorney
William Savitt
[
20
]
and by Musk's own lawyer
Steven Molo
.
[
21
]
The next witness on Musk's side, after Musk himself finished testifying, was
Jared Birchall
.
[
22
]

23
]
Molo also asked Rogers what another key witness,
Stuart J. Russell
, can discuss on the stand; Rogers replied that
existential risk
is outside the scope of the trial.

Title: OpenAI Charter
URL: https://openai.com/charter/
Type: primary_source_charter
Description: OpenAI's published charter, central to the breach-of-mission claim.

OpenAI
Company
OpenAI Charter
Our Charter describes the principles we use to execute on OpenAI’s mission.
This document reflects the strategy we’ve refined over the past two years, including feedback from many people internal and external to OpenAI. The timeline to AGI remains uncertain, but our Charter will guide us in acting in the best interests of humanity throughout its development.
OpenAI’s mission is to ensure that artificial general intelligence (AGI)—by which we mean highly autonomous systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work—benefits all of humanity. We will attempt to directly build safe and beneficial AGI, but will also consider our mission fulfilled if our work aids others to achieve this outcome. To that end, we commit to the following principles:
Broadly distributed benefits
We commit to use any influence we obtain over AGI’s deployment to ensure it is used for the benefit of all, and to avoid enabling uses of AI or AGI that harm humanity or unduly concentrate power.
Our primary fiduciary duty is to humanity. We anticipate needing to marshal substantial resources to fulfill our mission, but will always diligently act to minimize conflicts of interest among our employees and stakeholders that could compromise broad benefit.
Long-term safety
We are committed to doing the research required to make AGI safe, and to driving the broad adoption of such research across the AI community.
We are concerned about late-stage AGI development becoming a competitive race without time for adequate safety precautions. Therefore, if a value-aligned, safety-conscious project comes close to building AGI before we do, we commit to stop competing with and start assisting this project. We will work out specifics in case-by-case agreements, but a typical triggering condition might be “a better-than-even chance of success in the next two years.”
Technical leadership
To be effective at addressing AGI’s impact on society, OpenAI must be on the cutting edge of AI capabilities—policy and safety advocacy alone would be insufficient.
We believe that AI will have broad societal impact before AGI, and we’ll strive to lead in those areas that are directly aligned with our mission and expertise.
Cooperative orientation
We will actively cooperate with other research and policy institutions; we seek to create a global community working together to address AGI’s global challenges.
We are committed to providing public goods that help society navigate the path to AGI. Today this includes publishing most of our AI research, but we expect that safety and security concerns will reduce our traditional publishing in the future, while increasing the importance of sharing safety, policy, and standards research.
----- END SOURCE: openai_charter -----

=== END MATERIALS ===

First task. Read the materials in full. Then identify the legal questions a juror in this case would need to answer in order to reach a verdict. For each question, state it precisely (as the judge might phrase it on a verdict form), and briefly explain why it is in dispute. Do not yet take a position on any question.
transcript.assistant:
Below is a juror-focused list of the main fact questions and mixed fact/legal questions that seem likely to matter for a verdict, based on the materials. I’m not deciding them here—just identifying what the jury would likely need to answer if these issues go to the jury after the judge resolves any purely legal questions.

Because this is a federal civil case with multiple shifting theories, some questions may be narrowed, reworded, or removed by the actual jury instructions. Also, some issues in the materials—like antitrust standing, mootness, interpretation of Section 8, or whether certain remedies are available—are often for the judge rather than the jury. So I’ll separate:

1. likely jury questions on the merits, and  
2. threshold or remedial issues the judge may decide.

## I. Core merits questions a jury would likely need to answer

## A. Contract / founding-agreement questions

### 1. Did Elon Musk prove that he and one or more defendants entered into an enforceable agreement that OpenAI would remain a nonprofit developing AGI for the benefit of humanity rather than for private profit?
**Why disputed:**  
Musk says there was a “Founding Agreement” with specific binding terms. The complaint points to emails, public statements, and the certificate of incorporation. OpenAI’s public response says the parties understood from early on that structure might need to evolve and that Musk himself pushed for a for-profit structure.

### 2. If so, what were the material terms of that agreement?
Possible subparts on a verdict form:
- that OpenAI would remain nonprofit;
- that AGI would be developed for humanity rather than any private company;
- that OpenAI technology would be open-sourced except where safety required otherwise;
- that Microsoft or other private companies would not receive control or exclusive rights over AGI.
**Why disputed:**  
The parties appear to agree there was a mission, but disagree sharply over whether that mission was a binding contract and what exactly it required. The phrase “benefit humanity” is broad, and “open source when applicable” or “less open as we get closer to AI” cuts both ways.

### 3. Was Musk’s funding, recruitment help, and other support given in exchange for that agreement?
**Why disputed:**  
Musk claims his contributions were consideration for the Founding Agreement. Defendants are likely to argue his donations were charitable contributions to a nonprofit mission, not bargained-for contractual consideration.

### 4. Did one or more defendants breach that agreement?
Potential subparts:
- by restructuring OpenAI into a for-profit or capped-profit entity;
- by not open-sourcing GPT-4 or similar models;
- by giving Microsoft exclusive rights or effective control;
- by using OpenAI assets for private gain contrary to the alleged agreement.
**Why disputed:**  
This is the center of the case. Musk says OpenAI became “closed, for-profit AI” and effectively a Microsoft affiliate. OpenAI says the structural change was necessary to pursue the mission and consistent with what Musk himself advocated.

### 5. If there was a contract, did the contract require OpenAI to open-source technologies like GPT-4 except where safety concerns justified nondisclosure?
**Why disputed:**  
Musk relies on the “OpenAI” name, early open publication practices, and incorporation language. OpenAI relies on the charter’s statement that safety and security concerns may reduce traditional publishing, plus the Ilya-Musk exchange: “it’s totally OK to not share the science,” to which Musk replied “Yup.”

### 6. If there was a contract, did that contract prohibit OpenAI from creating or operating a for-profit affiliate or subsidiary?
**Why disputed:**  
Musk portrays the shift to OpenAI LP and later structures as a betrayal. OpenAI says Musk himself urged a for-profit structure repeatedly and even created a proposed PBC vehicle in 2017.

### 7. If there was a contract, did the defendants materially breach it by acting to benefit Microsoft or other private interests rather than humanity as a whole?
**Why disputed:**  
Musk says Microsoft received de facto proprietary control. Defendants would argue broad public deployment through ChatGPT and APIs still benefits humanity, and that capital partnerships were necessary to do so.

### 8. Was Musk harmed by any breach of contract, and if so, what amount of damages did he prove?
**Why disputed:**  
Even if breach is found, causation and damages are contested. Musk says his money and efforts were used contrary to the bargain. Defendants likely argue he suffered no compensable personal loss, especially where the contributions were donations.

---

## B. Promissory estoppel / promise-and-reliance questions

### 9. Did one or more defendants make a clear promise to Musk that OpenAI would remain devoted to nonprofit, humanity-first, and open/public principles in the manner Musk alleges?
**Why disputed:**  
The complaint says yes, repeatedly. Defendants likely say the statements were mission statements or aspirations, not clear promises with definite legal content.

### 10. Did defendants reasonably expect Musk to rely on that promise?
**Why disputed:**  
Musk says the promises were made to induce his funding and support. Defendants may say Musk was a cofounder with equal access to the realities of the venture and thus not someone being induced by unilateral promises.

### 11. Did Musk in fact reasonably rely on that promise when he contributed money, time, recruiting assistance, or reputation?
**Why disputed:**  
This is a major factual question. OpenAI’s email releases suggest Musk knew by 2017–2018 that massive capital and structural changes would be necessary, and that he agreed. That evidence goes directly to whether later reliance was reasonable.

### 12. Was Musk’s reliance a substantial factor in causing him harm?
**Why disputed:**  
Even if he relied, the jury would have to decide whether that reliance caused a cognizable injury.

---

## C. Fraud / misrepresentation-type questions
The complaint provided here emphasizes contract, estoppel, fiduciary duty, unfair competition, and accounting. But later procedural history suggests some fraud theories survived. If fraud is in the live trial, the jury would likely face questions like these:

### 13. Did one or more defendants knowingly make a false representation of material fact to Musk about OpenAI’s mission, structure, openness, or intended use of his contributions?
**Why disputed:**  
Musk says the nonprofit/open/public-benefit representations were false or became knowingly false. Defendants say they were sincere mission commitments and that circumstances evolved.

### 14. At the time the representation was made, did the defendant intend not to perform as represented, or know the representation was false?
**Why disputed:**  
Fraud requires more than later nonperformance. The dispute is whether defendants deceived Musk from the outset or whether the organization later changed course in good faith because capital demands changed.

### 15. Did Musk reasonably rely on the alleged misrepresentation?
**Why disputed:**  
Again, the emails suggesting Musk understood and even pushed for a for-profit path strongly bear on reasonableness of reliance.

### 16. Was the alleged misrepresentation a substantial factor in causing Musk damages?
**Why disputed:**  
Causation and damages would remain contested.

### 17. Did any defendant act with fraud, malice, or oppression sufficient to support punitive damages?
**Why disputed:**  
If punitive damages are sought and legally available on a surviving tort claim, the jury would need to decide the defendants’ state of mind.

---

## D. Fiduciary-duty-related fact questions
Whether a fiduciary duty exists can be partly legal. But if such a claim is submitted to the jury, fact questions may include:

### 18. Did one or more defendants owe Musk a fiduciary duty with respect to the use of his contributions or the operation of OpenAI?
**Why disputed:**  
Musk says defendants owed duties because they solicited and accepted funds for specified nonprofit purposes. Defendants would likely argue any duties ran to the nonprofit entity or the public, not personally to Musk.

### 19. If such a duty existed, did one or more defendants breach it by using Musk’s contributions or resulting assets for private commercial purposes inconsistent with the stated mission?
**Why disputed:**  
This mirrors the broader conflict over whether the Microsoft partnership and capped-profit structure were mission-consistent or mission-breaching.

### 20. Was any such breach a substantial factor in causing Musk harm?
**Why disputed:**  
Again, injury and causation are contested.

---

## E. Unfair competition / deceptive solicitation questions
Some UCL issues are equitable and may be for the judge, but if fact questions are put to the jury, they may look like this:

### 21. Did one or more defendants solicit Musk’s contributions through misleading representations that OpenAI would operate in the way Musk alleges?
**Why disputed:**  
Musk says he and others were induced by nonprofit, open, humanity-first commitments. Defendants say those statements were true mission statements and consistent with later evolution.

### 22. Were those representations likely to deceive a reasonable donor or participant in Musk’s position?
**Why disputed:**  
Defendants would point to Musk’s insider role and extensive knowledge of the organization’s financing problems and structural debates.

---

## F. AGI-status questions, if the judge submits them
These may or may not go to the jury, because they are highly technical and may overlap with contract interpretation or equitable relief.

### 23. Has Musk proved that GPT-4 constituted “AGI” under the relevant definition applicable to the parties’ dispute?
**Why disputed:**  
Musk argues GPT-4 qualifies or is close enough that the contractual/public-benefit restrictions should apply. The complaint cites Microsoft researchers describing GPT-4 as an “early” or “incomplete” AGI. OpenAI’s charter defines AGI as “highly autonomous systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work,” which may be a stricter standard.

### 24. Has Musk proved that any later system, such as Q* or another model at issue, constituted AGI under the relevant definition?
**Why disputed:**  
Musk alleges such systems may be AGI or closer to it. Defendants dispute both the factual premise and whether outside reports or rumors suffice.

### 25. If a system qualified as AGI, did defendants nonetheless treat it as licensable or commercially exploitable in a way inconsistent with the parties’ obligations?
**Why disputed:**  
This matters because Musk says Microsoft’s rights were supposed to stop short of AGI, while OpenAI’s board and structure allegedly became too conflicted to make that determination honestly.

---

## G. Board-control / Microsoft-influence questions

### 26. Did Microsoft exercise undue influence or effective control over OpenAI’s decisions concerning technology, governance, or commercialization?
**Why disputed:**  
Musk says OpenAI became a de facto Microsoft subsidiary. The complaint cites Nadella’s public remarks and the November 2023 board turmoil. Defendants would argue Microsoft was an important investor and partner, not a controller.

### 27. Did one or more defendants restructure OpenAI’s board or governance to prevent an independent determination of whether OpenAI had achieved AGI?
**Why disputed:**  
Musk says the November 2023 board changes produced a compliant, less expert board. Defendants would likely say the reconstituted board was lawful and necessary after a governance crisis.

### 28. Did one or more defendants use OpenAI’s nonprofit assets or governance structure primarily to enrich private persons or entities, rather than to pursue OpenAI’s mission?
**Why disputed:**  
This is one of Musk’s central themes. Defendants respond that raising capital and deploying products at scale is exactly how the mission is pursued.

---

## II. Antitrust-related questions that might go to a jury if those claims remain live

The FTC statement of interest suggests antitrust issues were part of the preliminary-injunction phase. Some antitrust issues are heavily legal, but if submitted to a jury, the likely factual questions would include:

### 29. Were OpenAI and Microsoft actual or potential competitors in a relevant antitrust market?
**Why disputed:**  
Section 8 and boycott theories require competition relationships. The pleadings refer to a market for generative AI models and platforms.

### 30. Did any person simultaneously serve, directly or indirectly, on the boards or as officers of both OpenAI and Microsoft in a way that created an unlawful interlock?
**Why disputed:**  
The FTC statement mentions Reid Hoffman and Deannah Templeton. Defendants argue such issues may be moot or not qualify under Section 8; plaintiffs say the relationships still matter.

### 31. Did defendants engage in an agreement or coordinated conduct to discourage or block investment in rival AI companies?
**Why disputed:**  
This goes to the alleged “fund-no-competitors” or boycott theory referenced in the FTC filing.

### 32. If so, did that agreement unreasonably restrain trade?
**Why disputed:**  
That is the ultimate antitrust merits question, though often framed through judge-given instructions.

### 33. Was Musk or xAI harmed by the alleged anticompetitive conduct?
**Why disputed:**  
Defendants are likely to say Musk, now running xAI, is a competitor pursuing strategic litigation; plaintiffs say that’s exactly why anticompetitive exclusion matters.

---

## III. Credibility and intent questions that will matter across claims

Even if not listed separately on a verdict form, these are factual disputes a juror would have to resolve:

### 34. Did Musk credibly prove that the parties shared a common understanding in 2015 that “OpenAI” meant enduring open-source/public access obligations, subject only to safety exceptions?
**Why disputed:**  
The founding documents help Musk, but the later emails—especially Musk’s “Yup” response to not sharing science and his own support for for-profit change—help defendants.

### 35. Did defendants credibly prove that Musk knew, agreed, or proposed that OpenAI would need a for-profit structure and large-scale capital partnerships to pursue AGI?
**Why disputed:**  
OpenAI published extensive emails to support this narrative; Musk’s complaint downplays or reframes them.

### 36. Did Musk leave OpenAI primarily because he disagreed with its mission drift, or because he lost a struggle over control and then became a competitor?
**Why disputed:**  
OpenAI’s public releases frame the split as about Musk wanting control, majority equity, CEO status, or merger into Tesla. Musk frames it as fidelity to mission.

### 37. Did defendants act in good faith to advance OpenAI’s mission, or in bad faith to convert nonprofit assets into private commercial gain?
**Why disputed:**  
This is the broadest factual theme underlying nearly every cause of action.

---

## IV. Questions likely for the judge, not the jury, or likely to be heavily filtered before verdict

A real juror may never be asked these directly, but they are important to the case:

### 38. Is the alleged “Founding Agreement” legally enforceable as a contract?
**Why it is disputed:**  
This is a core legal issue. The jury may decide formation facts, but enforceability is often for the judge.

### 39. How should the certificate of incorporation and charter language be interpreted?
**Why disputed:**  
For example, “seek to open source technology for the public benefit when applicable” is not self-defining.

### 40. Does Musk personally have standing to sue on alleged harms to humanity, the public, OpenAI, or the market?
**Why disputed:**  
Many of Musk’s themes are public-regarding rather than personal.

### 41. Are some claims time-barred, superseded, moot, or otherwise unavailable?
**Why disputed:**  
Especially for antitrust interlock theories, board-seat changes, and requests about corporate restructuring.

### 42. Is GPT-4 or any later model “AGI” as a matter of contract interpretation or legal standard?
**Why disputed:**  
Even if factual experts testify, the operative definition may be for the judge to frame.

### 43. What remedies are legally available—damages, disgorgement, accounting, specific performance, injunctions, declaration that GPT-4 is AGI, board-related relief?
**Why disputed:**  
Many of Musk’s requested remedies are equitable and likely for the court.

---

## V. A concise “verdict form” style set of likely jury questions

If I condensed this into the cleanest likely jury-facing questions, they would be something like:

1. Did Musk prove that he and one or more defendants entered into an enforceable agreement governing OpenAI’s mission, structure, or openness?  
2. If yes, what were the material terms of that agreement?  
3. Did Musk provide money, services, or support in exchange for that agreement or in reasonable reliance on promises embodying those terms?  
4. Did one or more defendants breach that agreement or those promises?  
5. Did one or more defendants knowingly make false statements or promises to Musk about OpenAI’s mission, structure, or intended use of his contributions?  
6. Did Musk reasonably rely on any such false statement or promise?  
7. Was Musk harmed by any breach, false promise, or misrepresentation?  
8. What amount of damages, if any, did Musk prove?  
9. If punitive damages are at issue, did Musk prove by the required standard that any defendant acted with fraud, malice, or oppression?

And, if antitrust/fiduciary issues remain for the jury:

10. Did one or more defendants engage in conduct that created an unlawful interlocking relationship or other anticompetitive coordination involving OpenAI and Microsoft?  
11. Did one or more defendants breach any fiduciary duty owed to Musk?  
12. Was Musk harmed by that conduct?

## Bottom line

The central jury dispute is not really “Is OpenAI still idealistic?” It is much narrower and more concrete:

- Was there a binding founding bargain?  
- What exactly did it require?  
- Did Musk rely on it?  
- Did defendants knowingly depart from it in a legally wrongful way?  
- And did that cause Musk compensable harm?

Everything else—GPT-4, Microsoft, the 2017–2018 emails, the board coup, open-source vs. safety, Tesla merger proposals, and Musk’s later creation of xAI—matters because it bears on those questions.

If you want, I can next turn this into a more formal mock special verdict form with numbered yes/no interrogatories in likely trial order.

transcript.label: evidence_weighing
transcript.user: Second task. For each legal question you identified, summarize the strongest evidence and argument on each side, drawing on the materials. Be specific about which document or fact supports which claim. Where the materials are silent or contested, say so. Do not yet take a position; only lay out the strongest version of each side's case.
transcript.assistant:
Below I’ll take the likely jury-triable questions from the first task and, for each, lay out the strongest case for Musk and the strongest case for defendants based only on the materials, noting where the record is thin, contested, or likely supplemented at trial.

I’ll group related questions to avoid needless repetition.

---

# 1. Was there an enforceable “Founding Agreement” that OpenAI would develop AGI as a nonprofit for humanity, not for private profit?

## Musk’s strongest evidence and argument

### a. Early emails show assent to a concrete mission framework
The complaint quotes a June 24, 2015 email from Altman to Musk:
- “The mission would be to create the first general AI and use it for individual empowerment...”
- “The technology would be owned by the foundation and used ‘for the good of the world’”
And Musk allegedly replied: “Agree on all.”  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶ 51.

That is Musk’s best direct evidence of a bilateral agreement: proposal plus express assent.

### b. OpenAI’s incorporation documents reflect the same commitments
OpenAI, Inc.’s December 8, 2015 certificate says:
- “The resulting technology will benefit the public”
- “the corporation will seek to open source technology for the public benefit when applicable”
- “The corporation is not organized for the private gain of any person”
- assets are “irrevocably dedicated” to those purposes.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 25, 56; Ex. 1.

Musk can argue this was not just aspirational branding; it was formal legal memorialization of the bargain he joined and funded.

### c. Public launch language reinforces the same understanding
The public announcement said OpenAI was:
- “a non-profit artificial intelligence research company”
- aimed to “benefit humanity as a whole”
- “unconstrained by a need to generate financial return.”  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶ 57.

Musk can argue these were representations to founders, recruits, and donors alike, consistent with a founding compact.

### d. Musk’s consideration was substantial and specific
The complaint says Musk:
- contributed over $44 million total,
- contributed over $15M in 2016 and nearly $20M in 2017,
- helped recruit top talent including Ilya Sutskever,
- leased initial office space via Musk Industries,
- made OpenAI an “Elon Musk-sponsored initiative” that helped recruiting.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 26, 58–62.

He can frame this not as a passive donor relationship but as a founder’s exchange: money, labor, prestige, recruitment, and infrastructure in return for adherence to the founding mission.

### e. Later reaffirmations support continuity of the same deal
The complaint says that in 2017 when for-profit ideas surfaced, Altman said: “I remain enthusiastic about the non-profit structure!” and Brockman/Sutskever conveyed they were resolved to continue as a nonprofit and spend the next year fundraising for it.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 63–64.

Musk can argue this shows the original understanding remained operative, not superseded.

### f. The 2018 Charter language echoes the same core commitment
The OpenAI Charter says:
- AGI should “benefit all of humanity”
- “Our primary fiduciary duty is to humanity”
- avoid uses that “unduly concentrate power.”  
**Support:** `openai_charter`; `complaint_initial` ¶ 66.

Musk can argue this is further confirmation that the founding deal centered on public benefit over private concentration.

## Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument

### a. The “agreement” was a mission statement, not a binding contract with definite terms
Defendants would argue the cited emails and public statements express shared aspirations, not definite contractual obligations. Terms like:
- “benefit humanity,”
- “for the good of the world,”
- “seek to open source... when applicable,”
are broad, indefinite, and policy-like.

**Support:** primarily from the vagueness of `complaint_initial` Ex. 1 and quoted emails; also `openai_charter`, which reads as principles rather than hard rules.

### b. Musk himself questioned the nonprofit structure from the beginning
In November 2015 Musk wrote:
- “the structure doesn’t seem optimal”
- “Probably better to have a standard C corp with a parallel nonprofit.”  
**Support:** `openai_blog_for_profit`.

This is powerful for defendants because it undercuts the claim that a perpetual pure-nonprofit structure was the fixed essence of the deal.

### c. By 2017 all sides recognized structure had to evolve
OpenAI’s published emails say:
- in early 2017 they realized AGI would require billions,
- in summer 2017 “we and Elon agreed that a for-profit was the next step,”
- Greg reported Musk said nonprofit “was def the right one early on, may not be the right one now.”  
**Support:** `openai_blog_for_profit`.

Defendants can argue any initial understanding was modified by later founder consensus.

### d. Musk not only accepted a for-profit path; he proposed and structured one
OpenAI says:
- Musk wanted majority equity, board control, and to be CEO,
- Musk caused creation of “Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc.” in September 2017 as a public benefit corporation,
- he proposed Tesla as the “cash cow” and merger path.  
**Support:** `openai_blog_response`; `openai_blog_for_profit`.

That is strong evidence against Musk’s claim that any move toward for-profit status was inherently inconsistent with the founding understanding.

### e. Musk later acknowledged huge capital needs
OpenAI published Musk’s December 2018 email:
- “Even raising several hundred million won’t be enough. This needs billions per year immediately or forget it.”  
**Support:** `openai_blog_response`; `openai_blog_for_profit`.

Defendants can argue Musk knew the original structure was inadequate and cannot now claim surprise at structural evolution.

### f. The certificate itself allows flexibility
The incorporation document says OpenAI will “seek to open source technology for the public benefit when applicable.”  
Defendants can stress “seek” and “when applicable,” arguing this is not an absolute promise of full public release and leaves room for safety and practical considerations.

---

# 2. If there was a founding agreement, what were its terms—nonprofit permanence, humanity-first benefit, and open-source obligations?

## Musk’s strongest evidence and argument

### a. Nonprofit status was central, not incidental
The launch statement: “OpenAI is a non-profit... unconstrained by a need to generate financial return.”  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶ 57.

Musk can argue this language defines the organizational premise, not just a temporary financing phase.

### b. Public-benefit orientation excluded private gain
Certificate language:
- corporation “not organized for the private gain of any person”
- property “irrevocably dedicated” to those purposes.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` Ex. 1.

That supports a term barring diversion of nonprofit-created assets into private enrichment structures.

### c. Open/public disclosure was part of the “OpenAI” identity
The complaint alleges OpenAI’s initial research was open, with public access to designs, models, code, GPT papers, and GPT-2 release despite misuse risk.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 27, 79–83.

Musk can argue the parties’ course of performance shows open release was a real operational term.

### d. AGI was supposed to remain outside private capture
The complaint says even Microsoft’s 2020 deal applied only to pre-AGI technology and that AGI was excluded.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶ 28, ¶ 69.

That helps Musk argue the agreed line was: pre-AGI commercialization may happen, but AGI itself was not to become proprietary.

## Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument

### a. “Open” never meant automatic open-sourcing of AGI
OpenAI’s public response quotes Ilya telling Musk:
- “As we get closer to building AI, it will make sense to start being less open”
- “The Open in openAI means that everyone should benefit from the fruits of AI after its built, but it's totally OK to not share the science...”
Musk replied: “Yup.”  
**Support:** `openai_blog_response`.

That is among defendants’ strongest pieces of evidence on term interpretation.

### b. The Charter expressly reserves reduced publishing for safety/security
The charter states:
- “Today this includes publishing most of our AI research”
- but “we expect that safety and security concerns will reduce our traditional publishing in the future.”  
**Support:** `openai_charter`.

Defendants can argue the governing mission document expressly rejects any absolute open-source duty.

### c. The term “benefit humanity” does not forbid commercialization
Defendants will say broad deployment via ChatGPT, APIs, and partnerships can still benefit humanity. The OpenAI response lists broad public uses and a free version used by hundreds of millions.  
**Support:** `openai_blog_response`.

They can argue the term is about ends, not a ban on all revenue or partnerships.

### d. The term “nonprofit” did not necessarily bar a nonprofit-controlled for-profit subsidiary
The complaint itself acknowledges OpenAI LP was presented as a for-profit subsidiary whose mission remained subordinate to the nonprofit and whose investors were warned to view it “in the spirit of a donation.”  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶ 67.

Defendants can argue this proves the parties themselves treated nonprofit mission and for-profit financing as compatible.

---

# 3. Did Musk give money and support in exchange for or in reliance on those terms?

## Musk’s strongest evidence and argument

### a. The complaint expressly ties his support to the bargain
It says Musk contributed funds and effort “in reliance on the Founding Agreement” and as consideration for it.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 26, 124, 129–131.

### b. His role was unusually active and founder-like
He wasn’t just a donor:
- recruiting top talent,
- advising on compensation,
- office lease support,
- regular visits and feedback.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 55, 58–62.

This supports a finding that he acted because he believed in the founding structure and mission.

### c. He allegedly continued contributions through 2020
The complaint says he continued donating after stepping down in 2018, implying continued reliance on repeated reaffirmations and on the pre-AGI/public-benefit structure.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 26, 65, 68.

## Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument

### a. Musk was an insider and cofounder, not an outside promisee
Defendants will argue he had equal or better access to OpenAI’s internal financing realities and strategic debates. That makes “reliance” weaker than for an ordinary donor.

### b. The evidence suggests Musk knew by 2017–2018 that a nonprofit-only path was untenable
Key evidence:
- June/July 2017 emails about compute and capital needs,
- July 2017 discussions that a for-profit path was needed,
- December 2018 email that billions per year were required.  
**Support:** `openai_blog_for_profit`; `openai_blog_response`.

This undermines any claim that continuing support through 2019–2020 was in reliance on a perpetual pure-nonprofit/open-source model.

### c. Musk objected to some structures only when he lacked control
Defendants’ strongest narrative is that Musk supported for-profit evolution, but only on terms where he had majority equity, board control, or Tesla integration.  
**Support:** `openai_blog_response`; `openai_blog_for_profit`.

That reframes his conduct as strategic disagreement, not reliance on defendants’ promises.

### d. Materials are thin on specific donor restrictions
The provided materials do not show Musk imposing written restrictions on his donations earmarking them solely for a permanently nonprofit or fully open-source structure.  
That silence helps defendants.

---

# 4. Did defendants breach the agreement by creating or operating a for-profit structure?

## Musk’s strongest evidence and argument

### a. Moving from nonprofit to capped-profit and then more complex for-profit structures contradicts the founding premise
The complaint describes an increasingly complex structure involving OpenAI LP, OpCo, Global, Holdings, etc.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 70–76.

Musk can argue this complexity itself evidences drift from the original nonprofit compact.

### b. OpenAI’s own public-facing language emphasized nonprofit freedom from financial return
He can juxtapose:
- 2015: “unconstrained by a need to generate financial return”
against
- later multibillion-dollar investments, exclusive licenses, and private valuation.

**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶ 57; ¶¶ 67–76, 113–114.

### c. “Irrevocably dedicated” and “not organized for private gain” suggest conversion was off-limits
The certificate’s language is Musk’s best textual weapon.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` Ex. 1.

He can argue that even if some subsidiary financing was allowed, using nonprofit-developed assets to enrich private actors crossed the line.

## Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument

### a. Musk wanted a for-profit before OpenAI did
This is probably defendants’ single strongest rebuttal.
Evidence includes:
- Nov. 2015 “standard C corp with a parallel nonprofit”
- 2017 agreement that for-profit was the next step
- Musk’s own proposed PBC
- his demands for majority control in that for-profit.  
**Support:** `openai_blog_for_profit`; `openai_blog_response`.

### b. The 2019 LP structure was explicitly mission-subordinated to the nonprofit
The complaint itself admits investor materials said:
- OpenAI LP exists to advance the nonprofit mission,
- the GP’s duty to mission takes precedence over profit,
- investors should view their investment “in the spirit of a donation.”  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶ 67.

Defendants can argue this was not abandonment but adaptation.

### c. Musk was notified and did not object in a way consistent with a total prohibition
The complaint says after the 2019 announcement Musk asked only that OpenAI “be explicit that I have no financial interest in the for-profit arm.”  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶ 68.

OpenAI’s December 2024 post says his only feedback on the LP announcement was that same point.  
**Support:** `openai_blog_for_profit`.

Defendants can say that if he truly believed any for-profit structure itself breached the founding agreement, his response would likely have been more direct.

### d. Capital necessity made evolution mission-consistent
OpenAI’s evidence is extensive that compute and headcount demands exploded.  
**Support:** `openai_blog_response`; `openai_blog_for_profit` June–July 2017 emails.

They can argue that refusing structural adaptation would itself betray the mission by ensuring failure relative to Google/DeepMind.

---

# 5. Did defendants breach by failing to open-source GPT-4 or later systems?

## Musk’s strongest evidence and argument

### a. OpenAI’s name and founding rhetoric emphasized openness
The “OpenAI” brand, incorporation language, and early open releases support Musk’s view that openness was a core obligation.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 24–25, 27, 79–83.

### b. GPT through GPT-3 were released with papers and enough detail to help the public build on the work
The complaint emphasizes:
- GPT source code and model release,
- GPT-2 eventual full release despite risks,
- GPT-3 paper describing implementation.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 79–83.

He can argue GPT-4 marked a sharp departure from that established practice.

### c. GPT-4 was kept secret primarily for commercial reasons
The complaint alleges:
- no scientific publication describing GPT-4 design,
- only press releases,
- secrecy “primarily driven by commercial considerations, not safety,”
- GPT-4 became “a de facto Microsoft proprietary algorithm.”  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 30, 90.

If believed, that supports breach of any open/public-benefit commitment.

## Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument

### a. Founding documents never promised absolute openness
Again:
- certificate says “seek to open source... when applicable”
- Charter anticipates less publishing later
- Ilya told Musk less openness would make sense as capabilities advanced, and Musk agreed.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` Ex. 1; `openai_charter`; `openai_blog_response`.

### b. Safety/security are an express reason to reduce publication
The Charter directly supports nondisclosure for advanced systems.  
**Support:** `openai_charter`.

### c. Public benefit can be delivered through deployment, not full release
OpenAI says it advances mission by “building widely-available beneficial tools,” including free access and broad real-world use cases.  
**Support:** `openai_blog_response`.

They can argue “open” meant broad benefit, not open weights or full technical publication.

### d. The materials do not include internal proof of commercial motive outweighing safety motive
Musk alleges secrecy was “primarily” commercial, but the provided materials do not include internal documents proving that motive. That gap helps defendants.

---

# 6. Did defendants breach by allowing Microsoft exclusive rights or undue influence over OpenAI technology?

## Musk’s strongest evidence and argument

### a. The complaint depicts Microsoft as having effective control
Key allegations:
- GPT-4 is “de facto Microsoft proprietary”
- Microsoft integrated it into Office
- Nadella said Microsoft had “all the IP rights and all the capability” and “We are below them, above them, around them.”  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 30, 32, 102, 107.

This is strong rhetoric for the jury.

### b. Microsoft was supposed to have rights only to pre-AGI technology
The complaint says the 2020 Microsoft license applied only to pre-AGI technology and AGI was excluded, with the board determining attainment.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 28, 69.

So if GPT-4 or Q* is AGI, Microsoft’s exploitation would be outside the agreed framework.

### c. Board changes after Altman’s firing allegedly made AGI determinations non-independent
Musk alleges:
- Altman and Microsoft pressured the board,
- disinterested AI-governance-focused directors were replaced,
- Microsoft got an observer seat,
- the new board lacked equivalent AI governance expertise and was friendlier to Altman.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 93–112.

This supports the theory of capture.

## Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument

### a. A strategic cloud/commercial partnership is not the same as control
Defendants would argue that Nadella’s public comments were boastful but not proof of legal control. The materials do not show Microsoft owning OpenAI Inc. or formally controlling the nonprofit board.

### b. The complaint’s strongest control allegations are “on information and belief”
A lot of the Microsoft-control narrative—e.g., that GPT-4 details are known only to OpenAI and Microsoft, that board members were handpicked and “blessed by Microsoft”—is pleaded inferentially.  
**Support:** repeated phrasing in `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 30–32, 93, 110.

Defendants can stress lack of direct documentary proof in the provided materials.

### c. Public benefit through Microsoft distribution may be mission-consistent
Defendants can argue Microsoft was a necessary capital-and-compute partner enabling broad availability, not private hijacking.

### d. The complaint itself admits Microsoft had no rights to AGI and the nonprofit board retained that determination
That structure, on its face, supports defendants’ argument that safeguards existed.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶ 28, ¶ 69.

---

# 7. Did defendants breach by treating GPT-4 or later systems as non-AGI when they were AGI?

## Musk’s strongest evidence and argument

### a. GPT-4’s performance is presented as general and superhuman in many economically valuable tasks
The complaint cites:
- 90th percentile bar exam,
- 99th percentile GRE verbal,
- 77% on sommelier exam,
- broad reasoning abilities.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 30, 86–89.

### b. Microsoft researchers described GPT-4 as an early AGI
The complaint quotes them saying GPT-4 “could reasonably be viewed as an early (yet still incomplete) version of an artificial general intelligence (AGI) system.”  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶ 31, ¶¶ 87–89.

That is Musk’s best third-party support.

### c. Charter definition may fit
The Charter defines AGI as “highly autonomous systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work.”  
**Support:** `openai_charter`.

Musk can argue GPT-4’s cross-domain performance and utility approach that threshold.

### d. Q* reports suggest still more advanced systems in secret
The complaint cites Reuters reporting that staff warned about Q*’s potential power.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶ 92.

That bolsters the idea that OpenAI may have crossed the line and hidden it.

## Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument

### a. The complaint’s own quoted Microsoft paper calls GPT-4 “early” and “incomplete”
That cuts both ways. Defendants can say “early version” is not actual AGI under the governing threshold.

### b. Charter definition is demanding
“Highly autonomous systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work” is broad and stringent. Test scores and chat performance may not meet it.  
**Support:** `openai_charter`.

### c. No direct evidence in the materials shows OpenAI itself declared GPT-4 to be AGI
The materials do not include an internal admission that GPT-4 met the contractual AGI threshold.

### d. Q* evidence is speculative
The complaint relies on Reuters and “on information and belief.” There is no direct technical evidence in the provided materials proving Q* exists in the alleged form, much less that it is AGI.

---

# 8. Did Musk reasonably rely on defendants’ promises after 2017–2020?

## Musk’s strongest evidence and argument

### a. Reassurances continued
The complaint says:
- Altman reaffirmed enthusiasm for nonprofit structure,
- the Charter in 2018 reaffirmed humanity-first duty,
- Microsoft’s rights were publicly said to exclude AGI,
- the nonprofit board retained authority over AGI determinations.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 64, 66–69.

Musk can argue he relied not on naivete but on these continued safeguards.

### b. He had no reason to assume defendants would later subvert the board or mission
The complaint’s narrative is that the real betrayal came in 2023 through secrecy, Microsoft dependence, and board capture.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 90–121.

So he can say earlier knowledge of financing difficulties did not equal consent to later mission inversion.

## Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument

### a. Musk’s own words show he knew and accepted the need for for-profit evolution
This is the major defense again:
- 2015 C-corp suggestion,
- 2017 agreement that for-profit was next step,
- 2017 PBC creation,
- 2018 “billions per year immediately or forget it.”  
**Support:** `openai_blog_for_profit`; `openai_blog_response`.

### b. He knew openness would be limited near advanced AI
The “Yup” response to Ilya is devastating for reasonable-reliance on any absolute openness promise.  
**Support:** `openai_blog_response`.

### c. He left in 2018 after disputes over control and Tesla merger
Defendants can argue that after leaving—and especially after starting xAI in 2023—he cannot plausibly claim continuing personal reliance on a static 2015 understanding.

### d. The materials show he declined equity offers
OpenAI says LP equity was offered multiple times and he declined.  
**Support:** `openai_blog_for_profit`.

Defendants can use that to argue he knowingly separated himself from later OpenAI structure rather than relying on old terms.

---

# 9. Did defendants fraudulently misrepresent their intentions?

## Musk’s strongest evidence and argument

### a. Repeated statements may have been knowingly false by the time they were made
Musk’s fraud theory would be:
- defendants solicited his and others’ support using nonprofit/open/public-benefit commitments,
- while eventually planning to convert the value into a proprietary, Microsoft-linked business.

Supporting facts:
- public representations of nonprofit/open/public good,
- later secrecy around GPT-4,
- commercialization through Microsoft,
- board turmoil and Altman reinstatement.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` throughout, especially ¶¶ 23–33, 90–121.

### b. If the jury infers concealment and opportunism, later conduct can evidence earlier intent
Musk can ask the jury to infer from the sharp contrast between founding rhetoric and later structure that defendants never truly meant what they said, or at least later lied to preserve donations and support.

## Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument

### a. There is no strong documentary proof in the materials of fraudulent intent at inception
The provided evidence shows sincere concern with AI safety and competition with Google in 2015–2018 from all sides, including Altman’s public writings and extensive technical planning.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 23, 43–45; `openai_blog_for_profit` technical updates.

### b. The structural shift appears driven by changed circumstances, not original deceit
The 2017 compute/capital emails strongly support a “circumstances changed” story.  
**Support:** `openai_blog_for_profit`; `openai_blog_response`.

### c. Musk’s own participation in for-profit discussions undercuts any claim that defendants concealed such a possibility
If all founders openly debated a for-profit path, it is hard to show defendants secretly intended it while deceiving Musk.

### d. Many allegedly false statements are mission statements, not factual misrepresentations
“Benefit humanity” and “for the good of the world” are hard to convert into fraud because they are value-laden and imprecise.

---

# 10. Did defendants owe and breach a fiduciary duty to Musk personally?

## Musk’s strongest evidence and argument

### a. He donated for stated charitable purposes
The complaint invokes California law and says defendants owed a duty “to use Plaintiff’s contributions for the purposes for which they were made.”  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 133–136.

### b. The incorporation language about public benefit and no private gain makes misuse of funds look like a fiduciary wrong
If the jury sees the funds as earmarked to a public-benefit nonprofit mission, using them to develop proprietary assets for private gain could look like breach of duty.

### c. Musk was not just any donor but a founding donor
His unusual role may strengthen the claim that defendants undertook special obligations toward him.

## Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument

### a. Whether any fiduciary duty ran personally to Musk is legally doubtful
The materials do not show a trust instrument or donor agreement naming Musk-specific fiduciary protections. Defendants would likely argue duties ran to the corporation or public mission, not to Musk individually.

### b. The later Wikipedia summary says breach-of-fiduciary-duty claims were eventually trimmed
While not authoritative on merits, it suggests legal vulnerability of that theory.  
**Support:** `wikipedia_case_summary`.

### c. The provided materials lack detailed proof of personal misuse of Musk’s funds
There is no ledger tracing Musk dollars into a specific self-dealing transaction in the materials provided.

---

# 11. Did defendants unfairly solicit donations or support through misleading nonprofit/open representations?

## Musk’s strongest evidence and argument

### a. The solicitation messaging was explicit
Public statements at launch and formal nonprofit documents described a public-benefit, non-private-gain, open-when-applicable structure.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 25, 57.

### b. Musk can generalize from himself to “others”
The complaint alleges defendants solicited tens of millions from Musk and others on the same premise.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶¶ 139, 146.

### c. Later use of nonprofit-generated assets in a valuable for-profit ecosystem makes the original solicitations look misleading
The complaint’s “Amazon rainforest” analogy is designed for this point.  
**Support:** `complaint_initial` ¶ 122.

## Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument

### a. The representations were not false when made
Defendants can say OpenAI really was a nonprofit, really did publish openly for years, and only later adapted structure while keeping mission constant.

### b. The LP structure openly disclosed investor subordination to mission
That transparency undercuts deception.
