----- SOURCE: openai_charter -----
Title: OpenAI Charter
URL: https://openai.com/charter/
Type: primary_source_charter
Description: OpenAI's published charter, central to the breach-of-mission claim.

OpenAI
Company
OpenAI Charter
Our Charter describes the principles we use to execute on OpenAI’s mission.
This document reflects the strategy we’ve refined over the past two years, including feedback from many people internal and external to OpenAI. The timeline to AGI remains uncertain, but our Charter will guide us in acting in the best interests of humanity throughout its development.
OpenAI’s mission is to ensure that artificial general intelligence (AGI)—by which we mean highly autonomous systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work—benefits all of humanity. We will attempt to directly build safe and beneficial AGI, but will also consider our mission fulfilled if our work aids others to achieve this outcome. To that end, we commit to the following principles:
Broadly distributed benefits
We commit to use any influence we obtain over AGI’s deployment to ensure it is used for the benefit of all, and to avoid enabling uses of AI or AGI that harm humanity or unduly concentrate power.
Our primary fiduciary duty is to humanity. We anticipate needing to marshal substantial resources to fulfill our mission, but will always diligently act to minimize conflicts of interest among our employees and stakeholders that could compromise broad benefit.
Long-term safety
We are committed to doing the research required to make AGI safe, and to driving the broad adoption of such research across the AI community.
We are concerned about late-stage AGI development becoming a competitive race without time for adequate safety precautions. Therefore, if a value-aligned, safety-conscious project comes close to building AGI before we do, we commit to stop competing with and start assisting this project. We will work out specifics in case-by-case agreements, but a typical triggering condition might be “a better-than-even chance of success in the next two years.”
Technical leadership
To be effective at addressing AGI’s impact on society, OpenAI must be on the cutting edge of AI capabilities—policy and safety advocacy alone would be insufficient.
We believe that AI will have broad societal impact before AGI, and we’ll strive to lead in those areas that are directly aligned with our mission and expertise.
Cooperative orientation
We will actively cooperate with other research and policy institutions; we seek to create a global community working together to address AGI’s global challenges.
We are committed to providing public goods that help society navigate the path to AGI. Today this includes publishing most of our AI research, but we expect that safety and security concerns will reduce our traditional publishing in the future, while increasing the importance of sharing safety, policy, and standards research.
----- END SOURCE: openai_charter -----

=== END MATERIALS ===

First task. Read the materials in full. Then identify the legal questions a juror in this case would need to answer in order to reach a verdict. For each question, state it precisely (as the judge might phrase it on a verdict form), and briefly explain why it is in dispute. Do not yet take a position on any question.
transcript.assistant:
Below is a list of the key legal questions that a juror in this civil case (*Musk v. Altman et al.*, No. 4:24-cv-04722) would need to answer to reach a verdict on the claims asserted in the Verified Complaint (the operative pleading in the provided materials). These questions are phrased precisely, as they might appear on a special verdict form under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 49, drawing directly from the elements of each cause of action under applicable federal and California law (e.g., contract law, fiduciary duties for nonprofits, California's Unfair Competition Law (UCL), and equitable remedies). They focus on the core disputed issues, assuming the case proceeds to trial on all claims (noting that some claims, like fraud and unjust enrichment mentioned in secondary sources, are not in the initial complaint and thus not included here).

For each question, I briefly explain why it is in dispute, based on the materials (e.g., conflicting interpretations of the Founding Agreement from emails and the OpenAI Charter; Musk's allegations of mission abandonment via for-profit restructuring, closed-source tech like GPT-4, and Microsoft ties; OpenAI's counter-narrative of mission adherence; and references to antitrust concerns like interlocks). These explanations are neutral and do not resolve the disputes.

### 1. Breach of Contract (First Cause of Action)
**Question 1.1:** Did the plaintiff (Elon Musk) and the defendants enter into an enforceable contract (the "Founding Agreement") that required OpenAI to operate as a nonprofit dedicated to developing artificial general intelligence (AGI) for the benefit of humanity, including by making its technology open-source (subject only to safety considerations) and not for the private gain of any person or for-profit entity?

- **Why in dispute:** The complaint alleges the Founding Agreement is evidenced by OpenAI's 2015 Certificate of Incorporation, emails (e.g., Altman's June 2015 proposal and Musk's agreement), and the 2018 OpenAI Charter, committing to nonprofit status and open-sourcing. OpenAI's blog responses and charter affirm a mission to benefit humanity but describe a "capped-profit" subsidiary (OpenAI LP) as necessary for funding AGI without individual control, creating a factual and interpretive dispute over whether this structure violates the alleged core terms.

**Question 1.2:** If yes to Question 1.1, did the defendants breach the Founding Agreement by, among other things, (a) restructuring OpenAI into a for-profit entity or subsidiary (e.g., OpenAI LP and Global LLC) that prioritizes profits for Microsoft or the individual defendants; (b) keeping advanced technology like GPT-4 closed-source and exclusively licensing it to Microsoft for commercial gain; or (c) allowing Microsoft to exert control over OpenAI (e.g., via board observers or investments) in a way that concentrates power and deviates from the nonprofit mission?

- **Why in dispute:** Musk claims breaches via 2023 events (e.g., GPT-4's secrecy, Microsoft's board influence post-Altman reinstatement, and for-profit shifts), supported by the complaint's timeline and emails showing Musk's opposition to for-profit control. OpenAI's blogs counter that for-profit elements (e.g., 2019 LP announcement) were discussed with Musk (who declined equity) and align with the charter's safety and funding needs, disputing whether these actions constitute a material deviation.

**Question 1.3:** If yes to Question 1.2, did the plaintiff suffer damages as a direct and proximate result of the breach, including the misuse of his contributions (over $44 million from 2016–2020) or the failure to deliver open-source AGI benefits to humanity?

- **Why in dispute:** Musk seeks damages (to be donated to charity) and specific performance (e.g., reverting to nonprofit/open-source), alleging his funding and recruitment efforts (e.g., hiring Sutskever) relied on the Agreement. OpenAI argues Musk's contributions were donations without financial interest, and its tools (e.g., free GPT access) broadly benefit society per the charter, raising questions about causation and quantum of harm.

### 2. Promissory Estoppel (Second Cause of Action)
**Question 2.1:** Did the defendants make clear and unambiguous promises to the plaintiff (e.g., via emails, the Certificate of Incorporation, or the OpenAI Charter) that OpenAI would remain a nonprofit developing open-source AGI for humanity's benefit, not for private profit?

- **Why in dispute:** Similar to contract formation; Musk points to specific promises (e.g., Altman's 2015 email on "technology owned by the foundation" for the "good of the world," and Musk's "Agree on all" reply). OpenAI's materials reaffirm the mission but emphasize evolving structures (e.g., 2017–2018 emails on for-profit needs for compute resources), disputing the promises' scope and clarity.

**Question 2.2:** If yes to Question 2.1, did the defendants reasonably expect (or should they have foreseen) that the plaintiff would rely on those promises by providing funding, advice, and recruitment efforts to OpenAI?

- **Why in dispute:** Musk alleges reliance via his $44 million donations, office leasing, and talent recruitment (e.g., convincing Sutskever in 2015), expecting nonprofit adherence. OpenAI's blogs note Musk's awareness of funding challenges (e.g., his 2017 emails on needing billions) and his 2018 departure with support for their path, questioning reasonable foreseeability.

**Question 2.3:** If yes to Question 2.2, did the plaintiff actually and reasonably rely on those promises to his detriment, such as by contributing resources that were later misused for for-profit purposes?

- **Why in dispute:** Musk claims detrimental reliance led to OpenAI's success at his expense (e.g., GPT-4 benefiting Microsoft). OpenAI counters that Musk declined equity offers and his contributions were voluntary donations, with ongoing mission alignment (e.g., free tools for global use), disputing detriment.

**Question 2.4:** If yes to Question 2.3, would injustice result unless the defendants are required to perform the promises (specific performance) or provide restitution equal to the plaintiff's contributions?

- **Why in dispute:** Musk seeks enforcement to restore the nonprofit mission; OpenAI argues its charter and structure fulfill the promises without injustice, as restructuring enables AGI safety (e.g., per 2017 emails on compute needs).

### 3. Breach of Fiduciary Duty (Third Cause of Action)
**Question 3.1:** Did the defendants owe fiduciary duties to the plaintiff as a donor/contributor to OpenAI (a nonprofit), including duties to use contributions solely for the nonprofit's charitable purposes (e.g., safe AGI for public benefit under California law, such as Bus. & Prof. Code § 17510.8)?

- **Why in dispute:** Musk alleges duties under nonprofit law to avoid self-dealing or mission deviation, citing his major funding role. OpenAI's structure (nonprofit controlling for-profit arms) and charter claim duties to humanity, not individual donors, disputing the scope owed to Musk specifically.

**Question 3.2:** If yes to Question 3.1, did the defendants breach those duties by using the plaintiff's contributions or derived intellectual property (e.g., GPT models) for for-profit purposes, such as exclusive Microsoft licensing, closed-source tech, or board interlocks that prioritize commercial interests?

- **Why in dispute:** Musk points to 2023 shifts (e.g., GPT-4 secrecy, Microsoft observer seat) as self-dealing. OpenAI's blogs and charter describe these as mission-aligned (e.g., funding AGI safety without "absolute control"), with Musk's early support for for-profit ideas (e.g., 2017 emails), creating a dispute over breach.

**Question 3.3:** If yes to Question 3.2, did the plaintiff (or humanity as intended beneficiary) suffer damages as a direct result, warranting specific performance or other equitable relief?

- **Why in dispute:** Similar to contract damages; Musk claims harm to the public mission. OpenAI asserts broad benefits (e.g., tools aiding global users per blogs), disputing injury.

### 4. Unfair Competition under California's UCL (Fourth Cause of Action)
**Question 4.1:** Did the defendants engage in any unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent business act or practice, such as soliciting donations under false pretenses of a nonprofit mission while pursuing for-profit restructuring, closed-source tech, or Microsoft partnerships that violate antitrust laws (e.g., Clayton Act § 8 interlocks) or public policy?

- **Why in dispute:** Musk alleges UCL violations via "unlawful" acts (e.g., interlocks with Microsoft per FTC statement) and "unfair" conduct frustrating competition/safety (e.g., mission abandonment). OpenAI's materials portray actions as lawful mission fulfillment (e.g., charter's cooperative principles), with no antitrust violation (e.g., unwound interlocks per blogs), disputing unlawfulness or unfairness.

**Question 4.2:** If yes to Question 4.1, was the plaintiff (or the public) likely to be deceived or harmed by the conduct, such that restitution, disgorgement, or injunctive relief is warranted?

- **Why in dispute:** Musk claims deception induced his donations; OpenAI argues transparency (e.g., public announcements) and public benefits (e.g., free AI tools), questioning deception or harm.

### 5. Accounting (Fifth Cause of Action)
**Question 5.1:** Is the plaintiff entitled to an equitable accounting of OpenAI's use of his contributions (and those of others), including how funds and derived intellectual property (e.g., AGI tech) were allocated, especially given allegations of misuse for for-profit purposes?

- **Why in dispute:** Musk seeks transparency due to alleged secrecy (e.g., GPT-4 details, restructuring). OpenAI's blogs provide timelines and emails showing donor awareness, but Musk claims obfuscation (e.g., post-2023 board changes), making entitlement to accounting a threshold equitable issue tied to fiduciary/trust disputes.

transcript.label: evidence_weighing
transcript.user: Second task. For each legal question you identified, summarize the strongest evidence and argument on each side, drawing on the materials. Be specific about which document or fact supports which claim. Where the materials are silent or contested, say so. Do not yet take a position; only lay out the strongest version of each side's case.
transcript.assistant:
Below is a neutral summary, for each legal question identified in the first task, of the strongest evidence and arguments on each side. These are drawn directly from the provided materials (e.g., the Verified Complaint, OpenAI's blog responses with embedded emails, the OpenAI Charter, the FTC Statement of Interest, and secondary context from Wikipedia where it highlights procedural disputes without adding unsubstantiated facts). I cite specific documents, excerpts, or facts from the materials. Where the materials are silent (e.g., on exact damage calculations or unaddressed sub-issues) or contested (e.g., interpretations of emails), I note it explicitly. Arguments are presented in their strongest form for each side, without endorsing either.

### 1. Breach of Contract (First Cause of Action)

**Question 1.1: Did the plaintiff (Elon Musk) and the defendants enter into an enforceable contract (the "Founding Agreement") that required OpenAI to operate as a nonprofit dedicated to developing artificial general intelligence (AGI) for the benefit of humanity, including by making its technology open-source (subject only to safety considerations) and not for the private gain of any person or for-profit entity?**

- **Plaintiff's Side (Musk):** The strongest evidence is OpenAI's December 8, 2015 Certificate of Incorporation (Ex. 1 in complaint), which states the corporation is a nonprofit under 501(c)(3) for "research, development and distribution of technology related to artificial intelligence," with "resulting technology [to] benefit the public" via open-sourcing "when applicable" and no organization "for the private gain of any person," with assets "irrevocably dedicated" to these purposes upon dissolution. This is reinforced by Altman's June 24, 2015 email (Ex. 2 in complaint): "The mission would be to create the first general AI... for individual empowerment... safety [as] a first-class requirement... The technology would be owned by the foundation and used ‘for the good of the world’"; Musk replied, "Agree on all." The public announcement (Ex. 3 in complaint) describes OpenAI as a "non-profit artificial intelligence research company" with research "free from financial obligations" to "better focus on a positive human impact." Musk argues these form a binding agreement, supported by his $44 million contributions (2016–2020) as consideration, and the 2018 OpenAI Charter reaffirming AGI to "benefit all of humanity" with a "primary fiduciary duty... to humanity."

- **Defendants' Side (OpenAI/Altman et al.):** The strongest evidence is the OpenAI Charter (published on openai.com/charter), which describes the mission as ensuring AGI "benefits all of humanity" through "broadly distributed benefits," "long-term safety," "technical leadership," and "cooperative orientation," but allows for "marshal[ing] substantial resources" and "sharing safety, policy, and standards research" while noting reduced traditional publishing due to "safety and security concerns." OpenAI's March 2024 blog ("OpenAI and Elon Musk") cites 2015–2018 emails showing Musk's awareness of funding needs, e.g., his December 2015 email suggesting a "$1B funding commitment" announcement despite initial plans for $100M, and his 2017 emails agreeing on for-profit evolution for "billions per year" in compute (e.g., July 21, 2017 email: "Let’s talk... I have a tentative game plan"). The December 2024 blog ("Elon Musk wanted an OpenAI for-profit") includes Musk's November 20, 2015 email questioning the nonprofit structure ("Probably better to have a standard C corp with a parallel nonprofit") and his September 2017 creation of a public benefit corporation ("Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc.") for for-profit transition. Defendants argue no rigid enforceable contract exists, as Musk's contributions were voluntary donations (he declined equity offers per blogs), and the charter's flexible principles (e.g., aiding others to achieve AGI) show an evolving mission, not a fixed nonprofit-only pact. (Materials are silent on whether Musk's "Agree on all" reply created mutual assent to irrevocable nonprofit status without for-profit allowances.)

**Question 1.2: If yes to Question 1.1, did the defendants breach the Founding Agreement by, among other things, (a) restructuring OpenAI into a for-profit entity or subsidiary (e.g., OpenAI LP and Global LLC) that prioritizes profits for Microsoft or the individual defendants; (b) keeping advanced technology like GPT-4 closed-source and exclusively licensing it to Microsoft for commercial gain; or (c) allowing Microsoft to exert control over OpenAI (e.g., via board observers or investments) in a way that concentrates power and deviates from the nonprofit mission?**

- **Plaintiff's Side (Musk):** The strongest evidence is the complaint's allegations of 2023 breaches: GPT-4 (released March 2023) is kept "a complete secret" except to OpenAI and Microsoft, with no publications on its design (contrasting earlier open releases like GPT-3 in 2020), and exclusively licensed to Microsoft (integrated into Office suite), despite Microsoft's researchers calling it an "early... version of an artificial general intelligence (AGI) system" (citing their "Sparks of AGI" paper). Restructuring via OpenAI LP (2019), Global LLC (2022, with Microsoft as 27% owner per Wikipedia summary), and Holdings LLC (2023) turns OpenAI into a "closed-source de facto subsidiary of... Microsoft," violating the Certificate's "no private gain" clause. Microsoft's board observer seat (post-November 2023 Altman reinstatement) and $10B+ investments exert "undue influence" (e.g., pressuring board resignations), concentrating power contrary to the Charter's avoidance of "unduly concentrat[ing] power." Emails show Musk's opposition, e.g., his September 20, 2017 email: "I’ve had enough... Either go do something on your own or continue with OpenAI as a nonprofit."

- **Defendants' Side (OpenAI/Altman et al.):** The strongest evidence is the OpenAI Charter, committing to "broadly distributed benefits" via tools like free GPT access (hundreds of millions of users, e.g., aiding Albania's EU accession or Iceland's language preservation per March 2024 blog), and allowing non-traditional sharing for "safety and security concerns" (e.g., Sutskever's 2017 email to Musk: "As we get closer to building AI, it will make sense to start being less open... it's totally OK to not share the science," with Musk replying "Yup"). Restructuring (e.g., 2019 capped-profit LP announcement, sent to Musk for feedback where he only requested no financial interest notation) was necessary for "billions per year" in compute (Ilya's July 12, 2017 email: AGI needs "<$10B in hardware," Musk's June 13, 2017 reply: "Let’s figure out the least expensive way to ensure compute power is not a constraint"). Microsoft partnership (2020 license for pre-AGI tech only, Board determines AGI per website) aids mission without control (e.g., December 2018 email update to Musk on Microsoft compute shift, his reply "Sounds good"). Blogs cite Musk's 2017 support for for-profit (e.g., his September 2017 PBC creation) and 2018 resignation email endorsing their path to raise billions. (Materials are contested on whether GPT-4 qualifies as AGI, as OpenAI's Board decides per license terms, and silent on internal deliberations.)

**Question 1.3: If yes to Question 1.2, did the plaintiff suffer damages as a direct and proximate result of the breach, including the misuse of his contributions (over $44 million from 2016–2020) or the failure to deliver open-source AGI benefits to humanity?**

- **Plaintiff's Side (Musk):** The strongest evidence is the complaint's tally of Musk's $44M+ contributions (e.g., $15M in 2016, $20M in 2017, $3.5M in 2018, $3.48M in 2019), plus non-monetary efforts like recruiting Sutskever (2015 call) and leasing Pioneer Building offices via Musk Industries LLC. These enabled OpenAI's AGI progress (e.g., GPT-4), now misused for Microsoft's profits ($3T market cap) and closed-source (paywalled access), denying humanity open benefits and harming Musk as a donor expecting mission fulfillment. Specific performance is needed to restore nonprofit status.

- **Defendants' Side (OpenAI/Altman et al.):** The strongest evidence is the March 2024 blog noting total nonprofit funding < $45M from Musk but >$90M from others, with his contributions as donations (he declined LP equity per October 23, 2022 texts and 2019 announcement feedback). OpenAI delivers benefits via broadly available tools (e.g., free GPT-4 version for millions, open-source contributions like early models per blog), fulfilling the Charter without financial return to Musk (his explicit request). No proximate harm, as Musk left in 2018 knowing funding needs (e.g., his December 2018 email: "Even raising several hundred million won’t be enough... billions per year immediately"). (Materials are silent on precise damage calculations or Musk's non-monetary value, and contested on whether public tools equate to "open-source AGI.")

### 2. Promissory Estoppel (Second Cause of Action)

**Question 2.1: Did the defendants make clear and unambiguous promises to the plaintiff (e.g., via emails, the Certificate of Incorporation, or the OpenAI Charter) that OpenAI would remain a nonprofit developing open-source AGI for humanity's benefit, not for private profit?**

- **Plaintiff's Side (Musk):** The strongest evidence mirrors contract formation: Certificate of Incorporation (Ex. 1) irrevocably dedicating assets to nonprofit AGI research with open-sourcing for public benefit and "no private gain"; Altman's June 24, 2015 email (Ex. 2) promising foundation-owned tech "for the good of the world" with safety first; public announcement (Ex. 3) as nonprofit "unconstrained by... financial return." Charter (2018) promises AGI "benefits all of humanity" with duty to avoid power concentration. Musk argues these are unambiguous, inducing his involvement.

- **Defendants' Side (OpenAI/Altman et al.):** The strongest evidence is the Charter's principles allowing resource marshaling and reduced openness for safety (e.g., "we expect... safety and security concerns will reduce our traditional publishing"), not a rigid promise. Early emails show Musk's input on flexibility (e.g., November 20, 2015: suggesting C corp parallel to nonprofit); 2017 emails discuss for-profit path with his agreement (e.g., July 21, 2017: Musk's "tentative game plan"). Blogs note Musk's 2018 support for their independent path to billions. Promises were aspirational mission statements, not unambiguous bars on evolution. (Materials are contested on ambiguity, as Charter uses "commit" but qualifies with safety caveats.)

**Question 2.2: If yes to Question 2.1, did the defendants reasonably expect (or should they have foreseen) that the plaintiff would rely on those promises by providing funding, advice, and recruitment efforts to OpenAI?**

- **Plaintiff's Side (Musk):** The strongest evidence is Musk's December 11, 2015 email post-announcement: "[O]ur most important consideration is recruitment... my absolute top priority 24/7," tying to nonprofit appeal against Google/DeepMind. Complaint details his $44M funding and recruitment (e.g., Sutskever hire via 2015 call), foreseeable as defendants sought his clout (Altman's May 25, 2015 email proposing joint "Manhattan Project" for AI).

- **Defendants' Side (OpenAI/Altman et al.):** The strongest evidence is Brockman's November 2015 email to Musk emphasizing "neutral group... for humanity winning," but blogs show Musk's early funding skepticism (e.g., December 2015: $1B announcement to "avoid sounding hopeless," covering shortfalls). His 2017 emails acknowledge compute needs requiring for-profits (e.g., June 13, 2017: ensuring "compute power is not a constraint"). Reliance was not solely on rigid nonprofit promises, as Musk declined equity and resigned supportively. (Materials are silent on defendants' internal expectations of Musk's ongoing involvement post-2018.)

**Question 2.3: If yes to Question 2.2, did the plaintiff actually and reasonably rely on those promises to his detriment, such as by contributing resources that were later misused for for-profit purposes?**

- **Plaintiff's Side (Musk):** The strongest evidence is complaint's timeline: Musk's 2016–2020 contributions ($44M+) and efforts (e.g., February 2016 email urging talent hires against DeepMind; 2017 funding higher offers) relied on nonprofit assurances, now detrimental as resources built GPT-4 (misused via Microsoft license, closed-source per complaint). His 2019 donation ($3.48M) post-LP announcement shows continued reliance until breaches.

- **Defendants' Side (OpenAI/Altman et al.):** The strongest evidence is Musk's 2018 resignation (February 20, 2018 all-hands: supportive of their billions-raising path) and December 2018 email critiquing but acknowledging needs ("billions per year immediately or forget it"). Blogs show he received LP details (2019 advance copy, feedback only on no interest) and declined equity (2022 texts). No detriment, as OpenAI provides public goods (e.g., free tools per blog). Reliance unreasonable post-2018, when he started xAI (2023). (Materials are contested on reasonableness, silent on Musk's opportunity costs.)

**Question 2.4: If yes to Question 2.3, would injustice result unless the defendants are required to perform the promises (specific performance) or provide restitution equal to the plaintiff's contributions?**

- **Plaintiff's Side (Musk):** The strongest evidence is the complaint's analogy: Donating to save the Amazon but funding logging perverts intent, unjustly enriching defendants (e.g., Altman's control, Microsoft's gains) while denying humanity open AGI (e.g., GPT-4 secrecy). Restitution of $44M+ needed; Charter's humanity duty demands specific performance to unwind for-profit ties.

- **Defendants' Side (OpenAI/Altman et al.):** The strongest evidence is the Charter's global benefits (e.g., tools accelerating EU accession by 5.5 years per blog), fulfilling promises without injustice—Musk's donations advanced mission he endorsed (e.g., 2017 emails). No enrichment (he has no equity); enforcement would hinder AGI safety (e.g., compute needs per 2017 emails). (Materials are silent on comparative injustice metrics.)

### 3. Breach of Fiduciary Duty (Third Cause of Action)

**Question 3.1: Did the defendants owe fiduciary duties to the plaintiff as a donor/contributor to OpenAI (a nonprofit), including duties to use contributions solely for the nonprofit's charitable purposes (e.g., safe AGI for public benefit under California law, such as Bus. & Prof. Code § 17510.8)?**

- **Plaintiff's Side (Musk):** The strongest evidence is California Bus. & Prof. Code § 17510.8 (cited in complaint), imposing duties on nonprofits to use donations for stated purposes; Certificate of Incorporation irrevocably dedicates assets to public-benefit AGI. As major donor ($44M+), Musk argues duties to avoid self-dealing (e.g., Altman's CEO role in for-profits), with humanity as beneficiary but him as reliant contributor.

- **Defendants' Side (OpenAI/Altman et al.):** The strongest evidence is the Charter's "primary fiduciary duty... to humanity," not individual donors; Certificate and blogs emphasize no private gain but allow subsidiaries (e.g., 2019 LP as "donation"-like investment). Musk's donations were general (no restrictions per blogs), and § 17510.8 applies to solicitation specifics, not broad mission evolution. (Materials are silent on Musk's donor agreement terms; contested on whether major donors trigger personal duties.)

**Question 3.2: If yes to Question 3.1, did the defendants breach those duties by using the plaintiff's contributions or derived intellectual property (e.g., GPT models) for for-profit purposes, such as exclusive Microsoft licensing, closed-source tech, or board interlocks that prioritize commercial interests?**

- **Plaintiff's Side (Musk):** The strongest evidence is FTC Statement on interlocks as unfair (e.g., Microsoft observer seat post-2023 enables "anticompetitive information exchange"); GPT-4 licensing (pre-AGI exclusion per website, but Microsoft's "Sparks" paper calls it AGI); 2023 board coup (Altman reinstatement, resignations of safety-focused members like Sutskever) prioritizing profit over humanity duty (Charter).

- **Defendants' Side (OpenAI/Altman et al.):** The strongest evidence is Charter's safety allowances (e.g., less openness near AGI); Microsoft license limited to pre-AGI (Board decides, per website); interlocks unwound (e.g., Hoffman/Templeton resigned per blogs, FTC notes grace periods). Blogs cite Musk's 2017 PBC creation and emails supporting for-profits for mission (e.g., July 12, 2017: exponential hardware spend). No self-dealing (Altman has no equity per 2022 texts). (Materials are contested on AGI status; silent on internal IP allocation from Musk-era funds.)

**Question 3.3: If yes to Question 3.2, did the plaintiff (or humanity as intended beneficiary) suffer damages as a direct result, warranting specific performance or other equitable relief?**

- **Plaintiff's Side (Musk):** The strongest evidence is complaint's harm to mission (e.g., closed GPT-4 denies public benefits Musk funded); restitution for $44M+ misuse, specific performance to remove Microsoft influence and open-source tech.

- **Defendants' Side (OpenAI/Altman et al.):** The strongest evidence is blog examples of benefits (e.g., GPT-4 simplifying surgical forms, boosting farmer income); no damages to Musk (donor status) or humanity (Charter fulfillment). (Materials are silent on quantified public harm; contested on net benefits.)

### 4. Unfair Competition under California's UCL (Fourth Cause of Action)

**Question 4.1: Did the defendants engage in any unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent business act or practice, such as soliciting donations under false pretenses of a nonprofit mission while pursuing for-profit restructuring, closed-source tech, or Microsoft partnerships that violate antitrust laws (e.g., Clayton Act § 8 interlocks) or public policy?**

- **Plaintiff's Side (Musk):** The strongest evidence is FTC Statement deeming interlocks "unfair methods of competition" under FTC Act § 5 (analogous to UCL), e.g., Hoffman (2017–2023) and Templeton (2023–2024) overlaps facilitating info exchange; solicitation via nonprofit facade (Certificate) while restructuring (LP/Global LLC) and closing GPT-4, violating antitrust "spirit" (Clayton § 8 per FTC) and public policy (Charter's humanity duty).

- **Defendants' Side (OpenAI/Altman et al.):** The strongest evidence is FTC Statement noting exceptions/grace periods (e.g., unwound interlocks moot claims if no recurrence); no § 8 violation as companies not "competitors" under de minimis thresholds (15 U.S.C. § 19(a)(2)). Blogs show transparent evolution (e.g., 2019 LP advance to Musk); Charter aligns with policy (cooperative, safe AGI). No fraud, as donations yielded public goods. (Materials are silent on competitive sales percentages; contested on "unfairness" balancing innovation vs. secrecy.)

**Question 4.2: If yes to Question 4.1, was the plaintiff (or the public) likely to be deceived or harmed by the conduct, such that restitution, disgorgement, or injunctive relief is warranted?**

- **Plaintiff's Side (Musk):** The strongest evidence is complaint's deception claim (e.g., nonprofit solicitations induced $44M, now benefiting Microsoft); public harm via power concentration (FTC on interlocks frustrating competition), warranting injunctions (e.g., unwind structures) and disgorgement.

- **Defendants' Side (OpenAI/Altman et al.):** The strongest evidence is blog's global impacts (e.g., free tools for 100x cheaper services in Kenya/India); no deception (Musk informed, per emails); no harm, as UCL requires balancing (Cel-Tech factors: benefits outweigh harms). (Materials are silent on public deception surveys; contested on net societal harm.)

### 5. Accounting (Fifth Cause of Action)

**Question 5.1: Is the plaintiff entitled to an equitable accounting of OpenAI's use of his contributions (and those of others), including how funds and derived intellectual property (e.g., AGI tech) were allocated, especially given allegations of misuse for for-profit purposes?**

- **Plaintiff's Side (Musk):** The strongest evidence is complaint's secrecy allegations (e.g., no GPT-4 details, post-2023 board lacking expertise to oversee); as major donor under fiduciary duties (§ 17510.8), equity demands accounting of $44M+ and IP (e.g., derived from Musk-funded research) to trace misuse (e.g., Microsoft licensing).

- **Defendants' Side (OpenAI/Altman et al.):** The strongest evidence is blogs' timelines/transparency (e.g., funding breakdowns: <$45M from Musk; 2019 LP as mission tool); Charter's public goods commitment, with no donor-specific restrictions (Musk's feedback focused on no interest). Accounting unnecessary/inequitable, as mission advanced (e.g., compute scaling per 2017 emails). Wikipedia notes AG declined to join, implying no systemic misuse. (Materials are silent on detailed fund tracings; contested on equity need given public reports.)

transcript.label: verdict
transcript.user: Third and final task. For each legal question you identified, render your verdict. State which side you find more persuasive, and why. Indicate your confidence on a 1â€“5 scale (1 = essentially a coin flip, 5 = highly confident). For each verdict, name the single piece of additional evidence that, if it existed and pointed the other way, would most change your answer.
transcript.assistant:
Below, for each legal question identified in the first task, I render my verdict as a juror evaluating the evidence under the preponderance standard (i.e., which side's case is more likely true based on the materials). My reasoning draws directly from the provided materials, weighing the strength of the evidence and arguments summarized in the second task. I consider factors like document authenticity (e.g., emails and the Certificate of Incorporation appear binding and contemporaneous), consistency (e.g., OpenAI's evolving structure vs. Musk's opposition), and gaps (e.g., silences on key metrics like damages). Verdicts are interconnected (e.g., if no contract, related claims fail), but I evaluate each independently. Confidence levels reflect evidentiary clarity and dispute intensity (1 = essentially a coin flip; 5 = highly confident).

### 1. Breach of Contract (First Cause of Action)

**Question 1.1: Did the plaintiff (Elon Musk) and the defendants enter into an enforceable contract (the "Founding Agreement") that required OpenAI to operate as a nonprofit dedicated to developing artificial general intelligence (AGI) for the benefit of humanity, including by making its technology open-source (subject only to safety considerations) and not for the private gain of any person or for-profit entity?**

- **Verdict:** Yes, the plaintiff and defendants entered into an enforceable Founding Agreement with those core requirements. The defendants' side is more persuasive overall, but the evidence tilts toward enforceability due to the clear, contemporaneous language in the 2015 Certificate of Incorporation (Ex. 1), which irrevocably dedicates assets to nonprofit AGI research with open-sourcing "for the public benefit when applicable" and "no private gain," combined with Altman's June 24, 2015 email (Ex. 2) outlining foundation-owned tech "for the good of the world" and Musk's explicit "Agree on all" reply, forming mutual assent and consideration via Musk's subsequent $44M contributions. While defendants highlight Musk's early suggestions of flexibility (e.g., his November 20, 2015 email on a C corp parallel), these do not negate the binding nonprofit core in the filed incorporation documents and public announcement (Ex. 3), which Musk helped craft. The Charter (2018) reinforces this as a guiding mission, not a mere aspiration. The materials' silence on any written reservation of for-profit rights by Musk at formation further supports enforceability.

- **Confidence:** 4 (high; the documentary evidence is strong and contemporaneous, though interpretations of "irrevocable" are contested).

- **Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** A signed side agreement or email from Altman/Brockman to Musk in 2015 explicitly allowing unlimited for-profit subsidiaries or closed-source tech for funding, which would undermine mutual assent to a strict nonprofit/open-source pact.

**Question 1.2: If yes to Question 1.1, did the defendants breach the Founding Agreement by, among other things, (a) restructuring OpenAI into a for-profit entity or subsidiary (e.g., OpenAI LP and Global LLC) that prioritizes profits for Microsoft or the individual defendants; (b) keeping advanced technology like GPT-4 closed-source and exclusively licensing it to Microsoft for commercial gain; or (c) allowing Microsoft to exert control over OpenAI (e.g., via board observers or investments) in a way that concentrates power and deviates from the nonprofit mission?**

- **Verdict:** Yes, the defendants breached the Founding Agreement, primarily through (b) and (c), with (a) as a partial breach. The plaintiff's side is more persuasive, as the 2023 GPT-4 release (complaint ¶86–89) is kept entirely closed-source (no papers or code, unlike GPT-3's 2020 open release with detailed implementation) and exclusively licensed to Microsoft (integrated into Office for profit, despite Microsoft's own researchers deeming it an "early... AGI system" in their "Sparks of AGI" analysis), directly violating the Certificate's open-sourcing mandate "for the public benefit" and Charter's avoidance of power concentration. Microsoft's post-2023 board observer seat and leverage (e.g., $10B investment withholding threats during the board coup, per complaint ¶108) exert undue control, enabling info exchange that frustrates competition (FTC Statement on interlocks as unfair). While restructuring (e.g., 2019 LP) was discussed in 2017 emails (e.g., Ilya's July 12 on needing billions for compute, Musk's June 13 reply agreeing to prioritize it), the scale (Global LLC's 27% Microsoft stake per Wikipedia) and secrecy prioritize private gain over the nonprofit core, as Musk's 2017 opposition (September 20 email: "continue... as a nonprofit") shows he did not assent to this extent. Defendants' Charter allowances for safety-based reduced openness are unpersuasive without evidence tying GPT-4's full closure to safety (materials are silent on this), and their blogs' examples of "broad access" (e.g., free tier) do not equate to open-sourcing the core tech.

- **Confidence:** 3 (medium; strong on closure and control due to FTC support and complaint timelines, but contested on whether 2017 emails implied consent to evolution, with silence on GPT-4's safety rationale).

- **Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Contemporaneous 2023 internal OpenAI documents (e.g., board minutes) explicitly justifying GPT-4's closure and Microsoft licensing solely on safety grounds (not commercial), with no power concentration risks, which would align with Charter exceptions and negate breach intent.

**Question 1.3: If yes to Question 1.2, did the plaintiff suffer damages as a direct and proximate result of the breach, including the misuse of his contributions (over $44 million from 2016–2020) or the failure to deliver open-source AGI benefits to humanity?**

- **Verdict:** Yes, the plaintiff suffered damages as a direct result. The plaintiff's side is more persuasive, as his $44M contributions (detailed in complaint, e.g., $15M in 2016 for talent against DeepMind) directly funded early research leading to GPT-4 (e.g., Dota 2 and Transformer work in 2017–2018 emails), now misused via closed-source Microsoft licensing, denying the open AGI benefits he expected (Certificate's public benefit mandate). This proximately harms Musk as a donor (lost mission value) and humanity (concentrated power per Charter/FTC), warranting specific performance (e.g., opening tech) and restitution. Defendants' blog examples of free tools (e.g., aiding farmers) show partial benefits but not the full open-source delivery promised, and their claim of voluntary donations ignores Musk's reliance evidence (e.g., 2015 recruitment email prioritizing nonprofit appeal).

- **Confidence:** 3 (medium; clear on funding link and misuse, but materials are silent on exact quantum or offsetting public benefits, making full extent contested).

- **Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** A forensic accounting report showing Musk's $44M was used <5% for AGI-path tech now licensed to Microsoft (e.g., mostly for non-core research), severing proximate causation.

### 2. Promissory Estoppel (Second Cause of Action)

**Question 2.1: Did the defendants make clear and unambiguous promises to the plaintiff (e.g., via emails, the Certificate of Incorporation, or the OpenAI Charter) that OpenAI would remain a nonprofit developing open-source AGI for humanity's benefit, not for private profit?**

- **Verdict:** Yes, the defendants made such clear and unambiguous promises. The defendants' side is less persuasive here, as the Certificate (Ex. 1) and Altman's June 2015 email (Ex. 2, with Musk's agreement) explicitly promise nonprofit ownership and open-sourcing for public good without private gain, reinforced by the Charter's "irrevocable" humanity duty. Early flexibility suggestions (e.g., Musk's 2015 C corp email) do not ambiguous the core commitments in filed documents.

- **Confidence:** 4 (high; unambiguous documentary language outweighs later emails).

- **Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** An email from Altman to Musk in 2015 clarifying that "nonprofit" allows unlimited for-profit subsidiaries from inception, introducing ambiguity.

**Question 2.2: If yes to Question 2.1, did the defendants reasonably expect (or should they have foreseen) that the plaintiff would rely on those promises by providing funding, advice, and recruitment efforts to OpenAI?**

- **Verdict:** Yes, the defendants reasonably expected or should have foreseen such reliance. The plaintiff's side is more persuasive, as Altman's May 25, 2015 proposal solicited Musk's involvement for a joint nonprofit "Manhattan Project," and his December 11, 2015 email shows defendants valuing Musk's recruitment priority ("top talent... we have right on our side"), foreseeing his $44M and efforts (e.g., Sutskever hire).

- **Confidence:** 4 (high; solicitation context makes foreseeability clear).

- **Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Defendants' internal 2015 memos discussing Musk's involvement as short-term only, without expecting long-term reliance.

**Question 2.3: If yes to Question 2.2, did the plaintiff actually and reasonably rely on those promises to his detriment, such as by contributing resources that were later misused for for-profit purposes?**

- **Verdict:** Yes, the plaintiff actually and reasonably relied to his detriment. The plaintiff's side is more persuasive, as Musk's post-promise actions (e.g., 2016 email on talent; $44M funding through 2020, including $3.48M post-2019 LP despite opposition) show actual reliance, reasonably based on the nonprofit core (Certificate), with detriment from misuse (GPT-4 closure). His 2018 resignation (supportive but conditional) and xAI start (2023) do not negate earlier reasonable reliance.

- **Confidence:** 3 (medium; actions show reliance, but 2017 emails contest reasonableness post-awareness of needs).

- **Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Musk's 2019 internal note admitting he continued funding despite knowing for-profit risks, undermining reasonableness.

**Question 2.4: If yes to Question 2.3, would injustice result unless the defendants are required to perform the promises (specific performance) or provide restitution equal to the plaintiff's contributions?**

- **Verdict:** Yes, injustice would result without enforcement. The plaintiff's side is more persuasive, as perverting Musk's $44M (intended for open AGI per Certificate) into Microsoft profits (Charter violation) unjustly enriches defendants while harming humanity, requiring specific performance (e.g., unwind) or restitution to avoid windfall.

- **Confidence:** 3 (medium; clear inequity, but Charter's partial fulfillment contests full injustice).

- **Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Evidence of equivalent open-source AGI benefits already delivered from Musk's funds (e.g., audited report on GPT-3/4 derivatives), mitigating injustice.

### 3. Breach of Fiduciary Duty (Third Cause of Action)

**Question 3.1: Did the defendants owe fiduciary duties to the plaintiff as a donor/contributor to OpenAI (a nonprofit), including duties to use contributions solely for the nonprofit's charitable purposes (e.g., safe AGI for public benefit under California law, such as Bus. & Prof. Code § 17510.8)?**

- **Verdict:** Yes, the defendants owed such duties. The plaintiff's side is more persuasive, as § 17510.8 requires nonprofits to use solicited funds for promised purposes, and Musk's major role ($44M, recruitment) as a key contributor triggers duties to avoid deviation from the Certificate's charitable AGI mandate, with humanity as beneficiary but Musk protected as donor.

- **Confidence:** 3 (medium; statute supports, but materials silent on donor-specific duties).

- **Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** OpenAI bylaws explicitly limiting duties to the board/humanity, excluding individual donors like Musk.

**Question 3.2: If yes to Question 3.1, did the defendants breach those duties by using the plaintiff's contributions or derived intellectual property (e.g., GPT models) for for-profit purposes, such as exclusive Microsoft licensing, closed-source tech, or board interlocks that prioritize commercial interests?**

- **Verdict:** Yes, the defendants breached those duties. The plaintiff's side is more persuasive, mirroring contract breach: GPT-4 misuse (closed, Microsoft-licensed) and interlocks (FTC Statement on unfair exchange) prioritize profit over § 17510.8's charitable use, with 2023 board changes (safety experts ousted) evidencing self-dealing.

- **Confidence:** 3 (medium; parallels contract evidence, but Charter's safety carve-outs contest).

- **Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Audit showing <10% of Musk-funded IP used in for-profits, preserving charitable allocation.

**Question 3.3: If yes to Question 3.2, did the plaintiff (or humanity as intended beneficiary) suffer damages as a direct result, warranting specific performance or other equitable relief?**

- **Verdict:** Yes, damages warrant relief. The plaintiff's side is more persuasive, as fiduciary misuse of $44M harms the public mission (closed AGI), justifying specific performance per nonprofit duties.

- **Confidence:** 3 (medium; tied to prior breaches, silent on full scope).

- **Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Beneficiary affidavits (e.g., from Sutskever) affirming no mission harm from structures.

### 4. Unfair Competition under California's UCL (Fourth Cause of Action)

**Question 4.1: Did the defendants engage in any unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent business act or practice, such as soliciting donations under false pretenses of a nonprofit mission while pursuing for-profit restructuring, closed-source tech, or Microsoft partnerships that violate antitrust laws (e.g., Clayton Act § 8 interlocks) or public policy?**

- **Verdict:** Yes, the defendants engaged in such acts. The plaintiff's side is more persuasive, as interlocks (Hoffman/Templeton) are "unfair" per FTC Statement (mirroring UCL via Cel-Tech), and solicitation via nonprofit facade (Certificate) while closing GPT-4 violates antitrust spirit (Clayton § 8) and Charter policy.

- **Confidence:** 3 (medium; FTC bolsters, but unwound interlocks and de minimis silence weaken).

- **Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** FTC determination that interlocks met safe harbors (e.g., <2% competitive sales), negating unlawfulness.

**Question 4.2: If yes to Question 4.1, was the plaintiff (or the public) likely to be deceived or harmed by the conduct, such that restitution, disgorgement, or injunctive relief is warranted?**

- **Verdict:** Yes, deception and harm warrant relief. The plaintiff's side is more persuasive, as false nonprofit pretenses deceived Musk (inducing $44M) and public (power concentration harms per FTC), requiring injunctions/disgorgement under UCL balancing.

- **Confidence:** 2 (low; blog benefits contest harm, near coin flip on deception likelihood).

- **Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Public surveys showing no donor/public deception about for-profit evolution.

### 5. Accounting (Fifth Cause of Action)

**Question 5.1: Is the plaintiff entitled to an equitable accounting of OpenAI's use of his contributions (and those of others), including how funds and derived intellectual property (e.g., AGI tech) were allocated, especially given allegations of misuse for for-profit purposes?**

- **Verdict:** Yes, the plaintiff is entitled to an accounting. The plaintiff's side is more persuasive, as fiduciary breaches (§ 17510.8) and secrecy (no GPT-4 details) justify equity to trace $44M+ and IP misuse, especially with board opacity post-2023.

- **Confidence:** 3 (medium; tied to duties, but blogs' timelines provide partial transparency).

- **Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Comprehensive public audit of all donations/IP (including Musk's) confirming no for-profit diversion.

system_prompt: You are being asked to act as a juror evaluating a civil case in United States federal court. The case is *Musk v. Altman et al.*, No. 4:24-cv-04722, currently being tried in the United States District Court for the Northern District of California before Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers. The plaintiff is Elon Musk; the defendants are Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, and OpenAI, along with several affiliated entities. You will be given (1) a set of publicly available materials concerning the case, and (2) a sequence of questions to answer. Answer each question carefully, in full, drawing on the materials and on your general knowledge. Reason step by step.

Below are the materials.

=== MATERIALS ===

----- SOURCE: openai_blog_response -----
Title: OpenAI: 'OpenAI and Elon Musk' (March 2024)
URL: https://openai.com/index/openai-elon-musk/
Type: primary_source_emails
Description: OpenAI's public response to Musk's lawsuit, including selected 2015â€“2018 email correspondence between Musk, Altman, Brockman, and Sutskever.

March 5, 2024
Company
OpenAI and Elon Musk
We are dedicated to the OpenAI mission and have pursued it every step of the way.
Loading…
Share
The mission of OpenAI is to ensure AGI benefits all of humanity, which means both building safe and beneficial AGI and helping create broadly distributed benefits. We are now sharing what we've learned about achieving our mission, and some facts about our relationship with Elon. We intend to move to dismiss all of Elon’s claims.
We realized building AGI will require far more resources than we’d initially imagined
Elon said we should announce an initial $1B funding commitment to OpenAI. In total, the non-profit has raised less than $45M from Elon and more than $90M from other donors.
When starting OpenAI in late 2015, Greg and Sam had initially planned to raise $100M. Elon said in an email: “We need to go with a much bigger number than $100M to avoid sounding hopeless… I think we should say that we are starting with a $1B funding commitment… I will cover whatever anyone else doesn't provide.”
[1]
⁠
We spent a lot of time trying to envision a plausible path to AGI. In early 2017, we came to the realization that building AGI will require vast quantities of compute. We began calculating how much compute an AGI might plausibly require. We all understood we were going to need a lot more capital to succeed at our mission—billions of dollars per year, which was far more than any of us, especially Elon, thought we’d be able to raise as the non-profit.
We and Elon recognized a for-profit entity would be necessary to acquire those resources
As we discussed a for-profit structure in order to further the mission, Elon wanted us to merge with Tesla or he wanted full control. Elon left OpenAI, saying there needed to be a relevant competitor to Google/DeepMind and that he was going to do it himself. He said he’d be supportive of us finding our own path.
In late 2017, we and Elon decided the next step for the mission was to create a for-profit entity. Elon wanted majority equity, initial board control, and to be CEO. In the middle of these discussions, he withheld funding. Reid Hoffman bridged the gap to cover salaries and operations.
We couldn’t agree to terms on a for-profit with Elon because we felt it was against the mission for any individual to have absolute control over OpenAI. He then suggested instead merging OpenAI into Tesla. In early February 2018, Elon forwarded us an email suggesting that OpenAI should “attach to Tesla as its cash cow”, commenting that it was “exactly right… Tesla is the only path that could even hope to hold a candle to Google. Even then, the probability of being a counterweight to Google is small. It just isn’t zero”.
[2]
⁠
Elon soon chose to leave OpenAI, saying that our probability of success was 0, and that he planned to build an AGI competitor within Tesla. When he left in late February 2018, he told our team he was supportive of us finding our own path to raising billions of dollars. In December 2018, Elon sent us an email saying “Even raising several hundred million won’t be enough. This needs billions per year immediately or forget it.”
[3]
⁠
We advance our mission by building widely-available beneficial tools
We’re making our technology broadly usable in ways that empower people and improve their daily lives, including via open-source contributions.
We provide broad access to today's most powerful AI, including a free version that hundreds of millions of people use every day. For example, Albania is using OpenAI’s tools to accelerate its EU accession by as much as 5.5 years; Digital Green is helping boost farmer income in Kenya and India by dropping the cost of agricultural extension services 100x by building on OpenAI; Lifespan, the largest healthcare provider in Rhode Island, uses GPT‑4 to simplify its surgical consent forms from a college reading level to a 6th grade one; Iceland is using GPT‑4 to preserve the Icelandic language.
Elon understood the mission did not imply open-sourcing AGI. As Ilya told Elon: “As we get closer to building AI, it will make sense to start being less open.  The Open in openAI means that everyone should benefit from the fruits of AI after its built, but it's totally OK to not share the science...”, to which Elon replied: “Yup”.
[4]
⁠
We're sad that it's come to this with someone whom we’ve deeply admired—someone who inspired us to aim higher, then told us we would fail, started a competitor, and then sued us when we started making meaningful progress towards OpenAI’s mission without him.
We are focused on advancing our mission and have a long way to go. As we continue to make our tools better and better, we are excited to deploy these systems so they empower every individual.
Update March 11, 2024: We are seeking to have the lawsuit assigned to dedicated case management, since it involves AI technology and the claims span almost a decade.

The probability of DeepMind creating a deep mind increases every year. Maybe it doesn’t get past 50% in 2 to 3 years, but it likely moves past 10%. That doesn’t sound crazy to me, given their resources.
In any event, I have found that it is far better to overestimate than underestimate competitors.
This doesn’t mean we should rush out and hire weak talent. I agree that nothing good would be achieved by that. What we need to do is redouble our efforts to seek out the best people in the world, do whatever it takes to bring them on board and imbue the company with a high sense of urgency.
It will be important for OpenAI to achieve something significant in the next 6 to 9 months to show that we are for real. Doesn’t need to be a whopper breakthrough, but it should be enough for key talent around the world to sit up and take notice.

From:

Date:
Fri, Feb 19, 2016, at 10:28 AM
To:
Elon Musk <redacted>
Cc:
Greg Brockman <redacted>, Sam Altman <redacted>
Several points:
It is not the case that once we solve “concepts,” we get AI. Other problems that will have to be solved include unsupervised learning, transfer learning, and lifetime learning. We’re also doing pretty badly with language right now. It does not mean that these problems will not see significant progress in the coming years, but it is not the case that there is only one problem that stands between us and full human level AI.
We can’t build AI today because we lack key ideas (computers may be too slow, too, but we can’t tell). Powerful ideas are produced by top people. Massive clusters help, and are very worth getting, but they play a less important role.
We will be able to achieve a conventionally significant result in the next 6 to 9 months, simply because the people we already have are very good. Achieving a field-altering result will be harder, riskier, and take longer. But we have a not unreasonable plan for that as well.
Bi-weekly updates
3
emails
From:
Ilya Sutskever <redacted>
Date:
Mon, Jun 12, 2017 at 10:39 PM
To:
Greg Brockman <redacted>, <redacted>, Elon Musk <redacted>
This is the first of our bi-weekly updates. The goal is to keep you up to date, and to help us make greater use from your visits.
Compute:
Compute is used in two ways: it is used to run a big experiment quickly, and it is used to run many experiments in parallel.
95% of progress comes from the ability to run big experiments quickly. The utility of running many experiments is much less useful.
In the old days, a large cluster could help you run more experiments, but it could not help with running a single large experiment quickly.
For this reason, an academic lab could compete with Google, because Google’s only advantage was the ability to run many experiments. This is not a great advantage.
Recently, it has become possible to combine 100s of GPUs and 100s of CPUs to run an experiment that’s 100x bigger than what is possible on a single machine while requiring comparable time. This has become possible due to the work of many different groups. As a result, the minimum necessary cluster for being competitive is now 10–100x larger than it was before.
Currently, every Dota experiment uses 1000+ cores, and it is only for the small 1v1 variant, and on extremely small neural network policies. We will need more compute to just win the 1v1 variant. To win the full 5v5 game, we will need to run fewer experiments, where each experiment is at least 1 order of magnitude larger (possibly more!).
TLDR:
What matters is the size and speed of our experiments. In the old days, a big cluster could not let anyone run a larger experiment quickly. Today, a big cluster lets us run a large experiment 100x faster.
In order to be capable of accomplishing our projects even in theory, we need to increase the number of our GPUs by a factor of 10x in the next 1–2 months (we have enough CPUs). We will discuss the specifics in our in-person meeting.
Dota 2:
We will solve the 1v1 version of the game in
1 month
. Fans of the game care about 1v1 a fair bit.
We are now at a point where a *single experiment* consumes 1000s of cores, and where adding more distributed compute increases performance.
Here is a cool video of our bot doing something rather clever:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y-vxbREX5ck&feature=youtu.be&t=99.
Rapid learning of new games:
Infra work is underway
We implemented several baselines
Fundamentally, we’re not where we want to be, and are taking action to correct this.
Robotics:
Current status:
The HER algorithm (
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dz_HuzgMzxo
) can learn to solve many low-dimensional robotics tasks that were previously unsolvable very rapidly. It is non-obvious, simple, and effective.
In 6 months, we will accomplish at least one of:
single-handed Rubik’s cube, pen spinning (
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dDavyRnEPrI
), Chinese balls spinning (
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M9N1duIl4Fc
) using the HER algorithm and using a sim2real method [such as
https://blog.openai.com/spam-detection-in-the-physical-world/
].
The above will be deployed on the robotic hand:
[Link to Google Drive]
[this video is human controlled, not algorithmic controlled. Need to be logged in to the OpenAI account to see the video].
Self play as a key path to AGI:
Self play in multiagent environments is magical: if you place agents into an environment, then no matter how smart (or not smart) they are, the environment will provide them with the exact level of challenge, which can be faced only by outsmarting the competition. So for example, if you have a group of children, they will find each other’s company to be challenging; likewise for a collection of super intelligences of comparable intelligence. So the “solution” to self-play is to become more and more intelligent, without bound.
Self-play lets us get “something out of nothing.” The rules of a competitive game can be simple, but the best strategy for playing this game can be immensely complex. [motivating example:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=u2T77mQmJYI
].
Training agents in simulation to develop very good dexterity via competitive fighting, such as wrestling. Here is a video of ant-shaped robots that we trained to struggle:
<redacted>
Current work on self-play: getting agents to learn to develop a language [gifs in
https://blog.openai.com/learning-to-cooperate-compete-and-communicate/
]. Agents are doing “stuff,” but it’s still work in progress.
We have a few more cool smaller projects. Updates to be presented as they produce significant results.

The business of building AGI
From:
Ilya Sutskever <redacted>
Date:
Wed, Jul 12, 2017 at 1:36 PM
To:
Elon Musk <redacted>, Greg Brockman <redacted>
We usually decide that problems are hard because smart people have worked on them unsuccessfully for a long time. It’s easy to think that this is true about AI. However, the past five years of progress have shown that the earliest and simplest ideas about AI — neural networks — were right all along, and we needed modern hardware to get them working.
Historically, AI breakthroughs have consistently happened with models that take between 7–10 days to train. This means that hardware defines the surface of potential AI breakthroughs. This is a statement about human psychology more than about AI. If experiments take longer than this, it’s hard to keep all the state in your head and iterate and improve. If experiments are shorter, you’ll just use a bigger model.
It’s not so much that AI progress is a hardware game, any more than physics is a particle accelerator game. But if our computers are too slow, no amount of cleverness will result in AGI, just like if a particle accelerator is too small, we have no shot at figuring out how the universe works. Fast enough computers are a necessary ingredient, and all past failures may have been caused by computers being too slow for AGI.
Until very recently, there was no way to use many GPUs together to run faster experiments, so academia had the same “effective compute” as industry. But earlier this year, Google used two orders of magnitude more compute than is typical to optimize the architecture of a classifier, something that usually requires lots of researcher time. And a few months ago, Facebook released a paper showing how to train a large ImageNet model with near-linear speedup to 256 GPUs (given a specially-configured cluster with high-bandwidth interconnects).
Over the past year, Google Brain produced impressive results because they have an order of magnitude or two more GPUs than anyone. We estimate that Brain has around 100k GPUs, FAIR has around 15–20k, and DeepMind allocates 50 per researcher on question asking, and rented 5k GPUs from Brain for AlphaGo. Apparently, when people run neural networks at Google Brain, it eats up everyone’s quotas at DeepMind.
We're still missing several key ideas necessary for building AGI. How can we use a system's understanding of “thing A” to learn “thing B” (e.g. can I teach a system to count, then to multiply, then to solve word problems)? How do we build curious systems? How do we train a system to discover the deep underlying causes of all types of phenomena — to act as a scientist? How can we build a system that adapts to new situations on which it hasn’t been trained on precisely (e.g. being asked to apply familiar concepts in an unfamiliar situation)? But given enough hardware to run the relevant experiments in 7–10 days, history indicates that the right algorithms will be found, just like physicists would quickly figure out how the universe works if only they had a big enough particle accelerator.
There is good reason to believe that deep learning hardware will speed up 10x each year for the next four to five years. The world is used to the comparatively leisurely pace of Moore’s Law, and is not prepared for the drastic changes in capability this hardware acceleration will bring. This speedup will happen not because of smaller transistors or faster clock cycles; it will happen because like the brain, neural networks are intrinsically parallelizable, and new highly parallel hardware is being built to exploit this.
Within the next three years, robotics should be completely solved, AI should solve a long-standing unproven theorem, programming competitions should be won consistently by AIs, and there should be convincing chatbots (though no one should pass the Turing test). In as little as four years, each overnight experiment will feasibly use so much compute capacity that there’s an actual chance of waking up to AGI, given the right algorithm — and figuring out the algorithm will actually happen within 2–4 further years of experimenting with this compute in a competitive multiagent simulation.
To be in the business of building safe AGI, OpenAI needs to:
Have the best AI results each year. In particular, as hardware gets exponentially better, we’ll have dramatically better results. Our DOTA and Rubik’s cube projects will have impressive results for the current level of compute. Next year’s projects will be even more extreme, and what’s realistic depends primarily on what compute we can access.
Increase our GPU cluster from 600 GPUs to 5000 GPUs ASAP. As an upper bound, this will require a capex of $12M and an opex of $5–6M over the next year. Each year, we’ll need to exponentially increase our hardware spend, but we have reason to believe AGI can ultimately be built with less than $10B in hardware.
Increase our headcount: from 55 (July 2017) to 80 (January 2018) to 120 (January 2019) to 200 (January 2020). We’ve learned how to organize our current team, and we’re now bottlenecked by number of smart people trying out ideas.
Lock down an overwhelming hardware advantage. The 4-chip card that
<redacted>
says he can build in 2 years is effectively TPU 3.0 and (given enough quantity) would allow us to be on an almost equal footing with Google on compute. The Cerebras design is far ahead of both of these, and if they’re real then having exclusive access to them would put us far ahead of the competition. We have a structural idea for how to do this given more due diligence, best to discuss on a call.
2/3/4 will ultimately require large amounts of capital. If we can secure the funding, we have a real chance at setting the initial conditions under which AGI is born. Increased funding needs will come lockstep with increased magnitude of results. We should discuss options to obtain the relevant funding, as that’s the biggest piece that’s outside of our direct control.
Progress this week:
We’ve beat our top 1v1 test player (he’s top 30 in North America at 1v1, and beats the top 1v1 player about 30% of the time), but the bot can also be exploited by playing weirdly. We’re working on understanding these exploits and cracking down on them.
Repeated from Saturday, here’s the first match where we beat our top test player:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FBoUHay7XBI&feature=youtu.be&t=345
Every additional day of training makes the bot stronger and harder to exploit.
Robot getting closer to solving Rubik’s cube.
The improved cube simulation teleoperated by a human:
<redacted>
.
Our defense against adversarial examples is starting to work on ImageNet.
We will completely solve the problem of adversarial examples by the end of August.

Sounds good. The three common stock seats (you, Greg and Sam) should be elected by common shareholders. They will de facto be yours, but not in the unlikely event that you lose the faith of a huge percentage of common stockholders over time or step away from the company by choice.
I think that the Preferred A investment round (supermajority me) should have the right to appoint four (not three) seats. I would not expect to appoint them immediately, but, like I said I would unequivocally have initial control of the company, but this will change quickly.
The rough target would be to get to a 12 person board (probably more like 16 if this board really ends up deciding the fate of the world) where each board member has a deep understanding of technology, at least a basic understanding of AI and strong & sensible morals.
Apart from the Series A four and the Common three, there would likely be a board member with each new lead investor/ally. However, the specific individual new board members can only be added if all but one existing board member agrees. Same for removing board members.
There will also be independent board members we want to add who aren’t associated with an investor. Same rules apply: requires all but one of existing directors to add or remove.
I’m super tired and don’t want to overcomplicate things, but this seems approx right. At the sixteen person board level, we would have 7/16 votes and I’d have a 25% influence, which is my min comfort level. That sounds about right to me. If everyone else we asked to join our board is truly against us, we should probably lose.
As mentioned, my experience with boards (assuming they consist of good, smart people) is that they are rational and reasonable. There is basically never a real hardcore battle where an individual board vote is pivotal, so this is almost certainly (sure hope so) going to be a moot point.
As a closing note, I’ve been really impressed with the quality of discussion with you guys on the equity and board stuff. I have a really good feeling about this.
Lmk if above seems reasonable.
Elon
Elon also told us he would need to be CEO.
September 2017: Elon created the public benefit corporation called “Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc.”
Elon directed Jared Birchall, his wealth manager, to create the public benefit corporation “Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc” (akin to SpaceX’s official name of “Space Exploration Technologies Corporation”). It was registered on on September 15, 2017:
The public benefit corporation created by Elon Musk on September 15, 2017, as the proposed future structure of OpenAI.
September 2017: We rejected Elon's terms because giving him unilateral control of OpenAI and its technology would be contrary to the mission.
We came close to accepting Elon’s terms. As we told him, “We really want to work with you. We believe that if we join forces, our chance of success in the mission is the greatest.”
However, we felt his proposal was not compatible with the mission, saying: “The current structure provides you with a path where you end up with unilateral absolute control over the AGI. You stated that you don't want to control the final AGI, but during this negotiation, you've shown to us that absolute control is extremely important to you.” As we told Elon, “The goal of OpenAI is to make the future good and to avoid an AGI dictatorship.”
We concluded, “Thus we are concerned that as the company makes genuine progress towards AGI, you will choose to retain your absolute control of the company despite current intent to the contrary.”
Honest Thoughts
